title: Ancient Egypt

infobox.value: *See [List of historical capitals of Egypt](/wiki/List_of_historical_capitals_of_Egypt "List of historical capitals of Egypt")*

infobox.key: • Unification of [Upper and Lower Egypt](/wiki/Upper_and_Lower_Egypt "Upper and Lower Egypt")
• [Annexation by the Roman Empire](/wiki/War_of_Actium "War of Actium")

infobox.value: was a [cradle of civilization](/wiki/Cradle_of_civilization "Cradle of civilization") concentrated along the lower reaches of the [Nile River](/wiki/Nile_River "Nile River") in the [eastern corner](/wiki/Northeast_Africa "Northeast Africa") of [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa"). It emerged from [prehistoric Egypt](/wiki/Prehistoric_Egypt "Prehistoric Egypt") around 3150 BC (according to [conventional Egyptian chronology](/wiki/Conventional_Egyptian_chronology "Conventional Egyptian chronology")),[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChronology2000-3) when [Upper and Lower Egypt](/wiki/Upper_and_Lower_Egypt "Upper and Lower Egypt") were united by [Menes](/wiki/Menes "Menes"), who is believed by the majority of [Egyptologists](/wiki/List_of_Egyptologists "List of Egyptologists") to have been the same person as [Narmer](/wiki/Narmer "Narmer").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDodsonHilton200446-4) The [history of ancient Egypt](/wiki/History_of_ancient_Egypt "History of ancient Egypt") unfolded as a series of stable kingdoms interspersed by the "[Intermediate Periods](/wiki/Periodization_of_ancient_Egypt "Periodization of ancient Egypt")" of relative instability. These stable kingdoms existed in one of three periods: the [Old Kingdom](/wiki/Old_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Old Kingdom of Egypt") of the [Early Bronze Age](/wiki/Early_Bronze_Age "Early Bronze Age"); the [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt") of the [Middle Bronze Age](/wiki/Middle_Bronze_Age "Middle Bronze Age"); or the [New Kingdom](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Egypt "New Kingdom of Egypt") of the [Late Bronze Age](/wiki/Late_Bronze_Age "Late Bronze Age").

**Ancient Egypt** was a [cradle of civilization](/wiki/Cradle_of_civilization "Cradle of civilization") concentrated along the lower reaches of the [Nile River](/wiki/Nile_River "Nile River") in the [eastern corner](/wiki/Northeast_Africa "Northeast Africa") of [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa"). It emerged from [prehistoric Egypt](/wiki/Prehistoric_Egypt "Prehistoric Egypt") around 3150 BC (according to [conventional Egyptian chronology](/wiki/Conventional_Egyptian_chronology "Conventional Egyptian chronology")),[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChronology2000-3) when [Upper and Lower Egypt](/wiki/Upper_and_Lower_Egypt "Upper and Lower Egypt") were united by [Menes](/wiki/Menes "Menes"), who is believed by the majority of [Egyptologists](/wiki/List_of_Egyptologists "List of Egyptologists") to have been the same person as [Narmer](/wiki/Narmer "Narmer").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDodsonHilton200446-4) The [history of ancient Egypt](/wiki/History_of_ancient_Egypt "History of ancient Egypt") unfolded as a series of stable kingdoms interspersed by the "[Intermediate Periods](/wiki/Periodization_of_ancient_Egypt "Periodization of ancient Egypt")" of relative instability. These stable kingdoms existed in one of three periods: the [Old Kingdom](/wiki/Old_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Old Kingdom of Egypt") of the [Early Bronze Age](/wiki/Early_Bronze_Age "Early Bronze Age"); the [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt") of the [Middle Bronze Age](/wiki/Middle_Bronze_Age "Middle Bronze Age"); or the [New Kingdom](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Egypt "New Kingdom of Egypt") of the [Late Bronze Age](/wiki/Late_Bronze_Age "Late Bronze Age").

The pinnacle of ancient Egyptian power was achieved during the New Kingdom, which extended its rule to much of [Nubia](/wiki/Nubia "Nubia") and a considerable portion of the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant"). After this period, Egypt entered an era of slow decline. Over the course of its history, it was invaded or conquered by a number of foreign civilizations, including the [Hyksos](/wiki/Hyksos "Hyksos"), the [Kushites](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kush "Kingdom of Kush"), the [Assyrians](/wiki/Assyrian_conquest_of_Egypt "Assyrian conquest of Egypt"), the [Persians](/wiki/History_of_Persian_Egypt "History of Persian Egypt"), and the [Greeks](/wiki/Ptolemaic_dynasty "Ptolemaic dynasty") and then the [Romans](/wiki/Roman_Egypt "Roman Egypt"). The end of ancient Egypt is variously defined as occurring with the end of the [Late Period](/wiki/Late_Period_of_Egypt "Late Period of Egypt") during the [Wars of Alexander the Great](/wiki/Wars_of_Alexander_the_Great "Wars of Alexander the Great") in 332 BC or with the end of the Greek-ruled [Ptolemaic Kingdom](/wiki/Ptolemaic_Kingdom "Ptolemaic Kingdom") during the [Roman conquest of Egypt](/wiki/Roman_conquest_of_Egypt "Roman conquest of Egypt") in 30 BC.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClayton1994217-5) In AD 642, the [Arab conquest of Egypt](/wiki/Arab_conquest_of_Egypt "Arab conquest of Egypt") brought an end to the region's millennium-long [Greco-Roman period](/wiki/Greco-Roman_period "Greco-Roman period").

The success of ancient Egyptian civilization came partly from its ability to adapt to the Nile's conditions for [agriculture](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_agriculture "Ancient Egyptian agriculture"). The predictable [flooding of the Nile](/wiki/Flooding_of_the_Nile "Flooding of the Nile") and controlled irrigation of its fertile valley produced surplus crops, which supported a more dense population, and thereby substantial social and cultural development. With resources to spare, the administration sponsored the mineral exploitation of the valley and its surrounding desert regions, the early development of [an independent writing system](/wiki/Egyptian_hieroglyphs "Egyptian hieroglyphs"), the organization of collective construction and agricultural projects, trade with other civilizations, and [a military](/wiki/Military_of_ancient_Egypt "Military of ancient Egypt") to assert Egyptian dominance throughout the [Near East](/wiki/Near_East "Near East"). Motivating and organizing these activities was a bureaucracy of elite scribes, religious leaders, and administrators under the control of the reigning [pharaoh](/wiki/Pharaoh "Pharaoh"), who ensured the cooperation and unity of the [Egyptian people](/wiki/Egyptians#Ancient_Egypt "Egyptians") in the context of [an elaborate system of religious beliefs](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_religion "Ancient Egyptian religion").[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJames20058Manuelian19986–7-6)

Among the many achievements of ancient Egypt are: the [quarrying](/wiki/Stone_quarries_of_ancient_Egypt "Stone quarries of ancient Egypt"), surveying, and construction techniques that supported the building of monumental [pyramids](/wiki/Egyptian_pyramids "Egyptian pyramids"), [temples](/wiki/Egyptian_temple "Egyptian temple"), and [obelisks](/wiki/List_of_Egyptian_obelisks "List of Egyptian obelisks"); a [system of mathematics](/wiki/Egyptian_mathematics "Egyptian mathematics"); a practical and effective [system of medicine](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_medicine "Ancient Egyptian medicine"); irrigation systems and agricultural production techniques; the first known planked boats;[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWard2001-7) [Egyptian faience](/wiki/Egyptian_faience "Egyptian faience") and [glass technology](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_technology#Glassworking "Ancient Egyptian technology"); new forms of [literature](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_literature "Ancient Egyptian literature"); and the [earliest known peace treaty](/wiki/Egyptian%E2%80%93Hittite_peace_treaty "Egyptian–Hittite peace treaty"), which was ratified with the [Anatolia](/wiki/Anatolia "Anatolia")-based [Hittite Empire](/wiki/Hittites "Hittites").[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClayton1994153-8) Its [art](/wiki/Art_of_ancient_Egypt "Art of ancient Egypt") and [architecture](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_architecture "Ancient Egyptian architecture") were widely copied and its [antiquities](/wiki/Egyptian_antiquities "Egyptian antiquities") were carried off to be studied, admired, or coveted in the far corners of the world. Likewise, its monumental ruins [inspired the imaginations](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt_in_the_Western_imagination "Ancient Egypt in the Western imagination") of travelers and writers for millennia. A newfound European and Egyptian respect for antiquities and excavations that began in earnest in the [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period") has led to much scientific investigation of ancient Egypt and its society, as well as a greater appreciation of its cultural legacy.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJames200584-9)

## History

Main articles: [History of ancient Egypt](/wiki/History_of_ancient_Egypt "History of ancient Egypt"), [Population history of Egypt](/wiki/Population_history_of_Egypt "Population history of Egypt"), [Sahara](/wiki/Sahara "Sahara"), [Northeast Africa](/wiki/Northeast_Africa "Northeast Africa"), and [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa")

The [Nile](/wiki/Nile "Nile") has been the lifeline of its region for much of human history. The fertile floodplain of the Nile gave humans the opportunity to develop a settled [agricultural economy](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_agriculture "Ancient Egyptian agriculture") and a more sophisticated, centralized society that became a cornerstone in the history of human civilization.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw200316,_65–66-10)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/timeline/remua5kx8cnr4cn4a9uvlogwifr3o3y.png)

### Predynastic period

Main article: [Predynastic Egypt](/wiki/Predynastic_Egypt "Predynastic Egypt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Predynastic_collage_%28new_version%29.jpg/250px-Predynastic_collage_%28new_version%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Predynastic_collage_(new_version).jpg)

Artifacts of Egypt from the prehistoric period, from 4400 to 3100 BC. First row from top left: a [Badarian](/wiki/Badari_culture "Badari culture") ivory figurine, a [Naqada II](/wiki/Naqada_culture "Naqada culture") jar, a [Bat](/wiki/Bat_(goddess) "Bat (goddess)") figurine. Second row: a [diorite](/wiki/Diorite "Diorite") vase, the *[Gebel el-Arak Knife](/wiki/Gebel_el-Arak_Knife "Gebel el-Arak Knife")*, a [cosmetic palette](/wiki/Cosmetic_palette "Cosmetic palette").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Gilf_Kebir_jan_2007_rock_carvings.jpg/250px-Gilf_Kebir_jan_2007_rock_carvings.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gilf_Kebir_jan_2007_rock_carvings.jpg)

Ancient [Petroglyphs](/wiki/Petroglyphs "Petroglyphs") of giraffe, ostrich, and longhorned cow being driven by a human, featured in the desert region, [Gilf Kebir](/wiki/Gilf_Kebir "Gilf Kebir"), Egypt.

In Predynastic and [Early Dynastic](/wiki/Early_Dynastic_Period_of_Egypt "Early Dynastic Period of Egypt") times, the [Egyptian climate was much less arid than it is today](/wiki/African_humid_period "African humid period"). Large regions of Egypt were [savanna](/wiki/Savanna "Savanna") and traversed by herds of grazing [ungulates](/wiki/Ungulates "Ungulates"). Foliage and fauna were far more prolific in all environs, and the Nile region supported large populations of [waterfowl](/wiki/Anseriformes "Anseriformes"). Hunting would have been common for Egyptians, and this is also the period when many animals were first [domesticated](/wiki/Domestication "Domestication").[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIkram19925-11)

By about [5500 BC](/wiki/6th_millennium_BC "6th millennium BC"), small tribes living in the Nile valley had developed into a series of cultures demonstrating firm control of agriculture and [animal husbandry](/wiki/Animal_husbandry "Animal husbandry"), and identifiable by their [pottery](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_pottery "Ancient Egyptian pottery") and personal items, such as combs, bracelets, and beads. The largest of these early cultures in upper (Southern) Egypt was the [Badarian culture](/wiki/Badarian_culture "Badarian culture"), which probably originated in the [Western Desert](/wiki/Western_Desert_(Egypt) "Western Desert (Egypt)"); it was known for its high-quality ceramics, [stone tools](/wiki/Stone_tool "Stone tool"), and its use of copper.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHayes1964220-12)

The Badari was followed by the [Naqada culture](/wiki/Naqada_culture "Naqada culture"): the Naqada I ([Amratian](/wiki/Amratian_culture "Amratian culture")), the Naqada II ([Gerzeh](/wiki/Gerzeh_culture "Gerzeh culture")), and Naqada III ([Semainean](/wiki/Semainean "Semainean")).[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKemp198914-13) These brought a number of technological improvements. As early as the Naqada I Period, predynastic [Egyptians](/wiki/Egyptians "Egyptians") imported [obsidian](/wiki/Obsidian "Obsidian") from [Ethiopia](/wiki/History_of_Ethiopia "History of Ethiopia"), used to shape blades and other objects from [flakes](/wiki/Lithic_flake "Lithic flake").[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAstonHarrellShaw200046–47-14)[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAston199423–26-15) Mutual trade with the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant") was established during Naqada II (c. 3600–3350 BC); this period was also the beginning of [trade with Mesopotamia](/wiki/Egypt-Mesopotamia_relations "Egypt-Mesopotamia relations"), which continued into the early dynastic period and beyond.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAtaç2014424–425-16) Over a period of about 1,000 years, the Naqada culture developed from a few small farming communities into a powerful civilization whose leaders were in complete control of the people and resources of the Nile valley.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChronology_of_the_Naqada_Period2001-17) Establishing a power center at [Nekhen](/wiki/Nekhen "Nekhen"), and later at [Abydos](/wiki/Abydos,_Egypt "Abydos, Egypt"), [Naqada III](/wiki/Naqada_III "Naqada III") leaders expanded their control of Egypt northwards along the [Nile](/wiki/Nile_Delta "Nile Delta").[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw200364–64-18) They also traded with [Nubia](/wiki/Nubia "Nubia") to the south, the oases of the [western desert](/wiki/Libyan_Desert "Libyan Desert") to the west, and the cultures of the [eastern Mediterranean](/wiki/Eastern_Mediterranean "Eastern Mediterranean") and [Near East](/wiki/Near_East "Near East") to the east.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw200361Ataç2014424–425-19)

The Naqada culture manufactured a diverse selection of material goods, reflective of the increasing power and wealth of the elite, as well as societal personal-use items, which included combs, small statuary, painted pottery, high quality [decorative stone vases](/wiki/Hand_drill_(hieroglyph) "Hand drill (hieroglyph)"), [cosmetic palettes](/wiki/Cosmetic_palette "Cosmetic palette"), and jewelry made of gold, [lapis](/wiki/Lapis "Lapis"), and [ivory](/wiki/Ivory "Ivory"). They also developed a [ceramic glaze](/wiki/Ceramic_glaze "Ceramic glaze") known as [faience](/wiki/Egyptian_faience "Egyptian faience"), which was used well into the [Roman Period](/wiki/Roman_Egypt "Roman Egypt") to decorate cups, amulets, and figurines.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholsonPeltenburg2000178–179-20)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFaience_in_different_Periods2000-21) During the last predynastic phase, the Naqada culture began using written symbols that eventually were developed into a full system of [hieroglyphs](/wiki/Egyptian_hieroglyphs "Egyptian hieroglyphs") for writing the ancient Egyptian language.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen20001-22)

### Early Dynastic Period (c. 3150–2686 BC)

Main article: [Early Dynastic Period of Egypt](/wiki/Early_Dynastic_Period_of_Egypt "Early Dynastic Period of Egypt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Narmer_Palette.jpg/250px-Narmer_Palette.jpg)](/wiki/File:Narmer_Palette.jpg)

The [Narmer Palette](/wiki/Narmer_Palette "Narmer Palette") depicts the unification of the Two Lands.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobins200832-23)

The Early Dynastic Period was approximately contemporary to the early [Sumerian](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer")-[Akkadian](/wiki/Akkadian_Empire "Akkadian Empire") civilization of [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") and of ancient [Elam](/wiki/Elam "Elam"). The third-century BC Egyptian priest [Manetho](/wiki/Manetho "Manetho") grouped the long line of kings from [Menes](/wiki/Menes "Menes") to his own time into 30 dynasties, a system still used today. He began his official history with the king named "Meni" (or Menes in Greek), who was believed to have united the two kingdoms of [Upper](/wiki/Upper_Egypt "Upper Egypt") and [Lower Egypt](/wiki/Lower_Egypt "Lower Egypt").[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClayton19946-24)

The transition to a unified state happened more gradually than ancient Egyptian writers represented, and there is no contemporary record of Menes. Some scholars now believe, however, that the mythical Menes may have been the king [Narmer](/wiki/Narmer "Narmer"), who is depicted wearing [royal regalia](/wiki/Regalia_of_the_Pharaoh "Regalia of the Pharaoh") on the ceremonial *[Narmer Palette](/wiki/Narmer_Palette "Narmer Palette")*, in a symbolic act of unification.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClayton199412–13-25) In the Early Dynastic Period, which began about 3000 BC, the first of the Dynastic kings solidified control over Lower Egypt by establishing a capital at [Memphis](/wiki/Memphis,_Egypt "Memphis, Egypt"), from which he could control the [labor force](/wiki/Labor_force "Labor force") and agriculture of the fertile [delta region](/wiki/Nile_delta "Nile delta"), as well as the lucrative and critical [trade routes](/wiki/Trade_route "Trade route") to the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant"). The increasing power and wealth of the kings during the early dynastic period was reflected in their elaborate [mastaba](/wiki/Mastaba "Mastaba") tombs and [mortuary cult](/wiki/Mortuary_cult "Mortuary cult") structures at Abydos, which were used to celebrate the [deified king](/w/index.php?title=Deified_king&action=edit&redlink=1 "Deified king (page does not exist)") after his death.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw200366–67-26) The strong institution of kingship developed by the kings served to legitimize state control over the land, labor, and resources that were essential to the survival and growth of ancient Egyptian civilization.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarly_Dynastic_Egypt2001-27)

### Old Kingdom (2686–2181 BC)

Main article: [Old Kingdom of Egypt](/wiki/Old_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Old Kingdom of Egypt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/All_Gizah_Pyramids.jpg/250px-All_Gizah_Pyramids.jpg)](/wiki/File:All_Gizah_Pyramids.jpg)

The [pyramids of Giza](/wiki/Giza_pyramid_complex "Giza pyramid complex") are among the most recognizable symbols of ancient Egyptian civilization.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Khafre_statue.jpg/250px-Khafre_statue.jpg)](/wiki/File:Khafre_statue.jpg)

[Khafre enthroned](/wiki/Khafre_enthroned "Khafre enthroned") (c. 2558–2532 BC)

Major advances in architecture, art, and technology were made during the [Old Kingdom](/wiki/Old_Kingdom "Old Kingdom"), fueled by the increased [agricultural productivity](/wiki/Agricultural_productivity "Agricultural productivity") and resulting population growth, made possible by a well-developed central administration.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJames200540-28) Some of ancient Egypt's crowning achievements, the [Giza pyramids](/wiki/Giza_Necropolis "Giza Necropolis") and [Great Sphinx](/wiki/Great_Sphinx_of_Giza "Great Sphinx of Giza"), were constructed during the Old Kingdom. Under the direction of the [vizier](/wiki/Vizier_(Ancient_Egypt) "Vizier (Ancient Egypt)"), state officials collected taxes, coordinated irrigation projects to improve [crop yield](/wiki/Crop_yield "Crop yield"), and drafted peasants to work on construction projects.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw200393–95-29)

With the rise of central administration in Egypt, a new class of educated scribes and officials emerged and were granted estates by the king as payment for their services. Kings also made land grants to their mortuary cults and local [temples](/wiki/Egyptian_temple "Egyptian temple"), to ensure that these institutions had the resources to worship the king after his death. Scholars believe that five centuries of these practices slowly eroded the economic vitality of Egypt, and that the economy could no longer afford to support a large centralized administration.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw2003104–107-30) As the power of the kings diminished, regional governors called [nomarchs](/wiki/Nomarch "Nomarch") began to challenge the supremacy of the office of king. This, coupled with [severe droughts](/wiki/4.2_kiloyear_event "4.2 kiloyear event") between 2200 and 2150 BC,[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHassan2011-31) is believed to have caused the country to enter the 140-year period of famine and strife known as the First Intermediate Period.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClayton199469-32)

### First Intermediate Period (2181–2055 BC)

Main article: [First Intermediate Period of Egypt](/wiki/First_Intermediate_Period_of_Egypt "First Intermediate Period of Egypt")

After Egypt's [central government](/wiki/Central_government "Central government") collapsed at the end of the Old Kingdom, the administration could no longer support or stabilize the country's economy. The ensuing food shortages and political disputes escalated into famines and small-scale civil wars. Yet despite difficult problems, local leaders, owing no tribute to the king, used their new-found independence to establish a thriving culture in the provinces. Once in control of their own resources, the provinces became economically richer—which was demonstrated by larger and better burials among all social classes.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw2003111–112-33)

Free from their loyalties to the king, local rulers began competing with each other for territorial control and [political power](/wiki/Political_power "Political power"). By 2160 BC, rulers in [Herakleopolis](/wiki/Heracleopolis_Magna "Heracleopolis Magna") controlled Lower Egypt in the north, while a rival clan based in [Thebes](/wiki/Thebes,_Egypt "Thebes, Egypt"), the [Intef family](/wiki/Intef_I "Intef I"), took control of Upper Egypt in the south. As the Intefs grew in power and expanded their control northward, a clash between the two rival dynasties became inevitable. Around 2055 BC the northern Theban forces under [Nebhepetre Mentuhotep II](/wiki/Mentuhotep_II "Mentuhotep II") finally defeated the Herakleopolitan rulers, reuniting the Two Lands. They inaugurated a period of economic and cultural renaissance known as the [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt").[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClayton199429-34)

### Middle Kingdom (2134–1690 BC)

Main article: [Middle Kingdom of Egypt](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/GuardianStatueofAmenemhmatII.jpg/250px-GuardianStatueofAmenemhmatII.jpg)](/wiki/File:GuardianStatueofAmenemhmatII.jpg)

A figure wearing the red crown of Lower Egypt, most probably [Amenemhat II](/wiki/Amenemhat_II "Amenemhat II") or [Senwosret II](/wiki/Senwosret_II "Senwosret II")

The kings of the Middle Kingdom restored the country's stability, which saw a resurgence of art and monumental building projects, and a new flourishing of [literature](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_literature "Ancient Egyptian literature").[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw2003142,_171-35) Mentuhotep II and his [Eleventh Dynasty](/wiki/Eleventh_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Eleventh Dynasty of Egypt") successors ruled from Thebes, but the vizier [Amenemhat I](/wiki/Amenemhat_I "Amenemhat I"), upon assuming the kingship at the beginning of the [Twelfth Dynasty](/wiki/Twelfth_dynasty_of_Egypt "Twelfth dynasty of Egypt") around 1985 BC, shifted the kingdom's capital to the city of [Itjtawy](/wiki/Itjtawy "Itjtawy"), located in [Faiyum](/wiki/Faiyum_Oasis "Faiyum Oasis").[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClayton199479-36) From Itjtawy, the kings of the Twelfth Dynasty undertook a far-sighted [land reclamation](/wiki/Land_reclamation "Land reclamation") and irrigation scheme to increase agricultural output in the region. Moreover, the military reconquered territory in [Nubia](/wiki/Nubia "Nubia") that was rich in quarries and gold mines, while laborers built a defensive structure in the Eastern Delta, called the "[Walls of the Ruler](/wiki/Walls_of_the_Ruler "Walls of the Ruler")", to defend against foreign attack.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw2003148–152-37)

With the kings having secured the country militarily and politically and with vast agricultural and mineral wealth at their disposal, the nation's population, arts, and religion flourished. The Middle Kingdom displayed an increase in expressions of personal piety toward the gods. Middle Kingdom literature featured sophisticated themes and characters written in a confident, eloquent style.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw2003169–171-38) The [relief](/wiki/Relief "Relief") and portrait sculpture of the period captured subtle, individual details that reached new heights of technical sophistication.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobins200890-39)

### Second Intermediate Period (1674–1549 BC) and the Hyksos

Main article: [Second Intermediate Period of Egypt](/wiki/Second_Intermediate_Period_of_Egypt "Second Intermediate Period of Egypt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Ceremonial_axe_of_Ahmose_I_%28both_sides%29.jpg/250px-Ceremonial_axe_of_Ahmose_I_%28both_sides%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ceremonial_axe_of_Ahmose_I_(both_sides).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Pharaoh_Ahmose_I_slaying_a_Hyksos_%28axe_of_Ahmose_I%2C_from_the_Treasure_of_Queen_Aahhotep_II%29_Colorized_per_source.jpg/250px-Pharaoh_Ahmose_I_slaying_a_Hyksos_%28axe_of_Ahmose_I%2C_from_the_Treasure_of_Queen_Aahhotep_II%29_Colorized_per_source.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pharaoh_Ahmose_I_slaying_a_Hyksos_(axe_of_Ahmose_I,_from_the_Treasure_of_Queen_Aahhotep_II)_Colorized_per_source.jpg)

Pharaoh [Ahmose I](/wiki/Ahmose_I "Ahmose I") (ruled c. 1549–1524 BC) slaying a probable [Hyksos](/wiki/Hyksos "Hyksos"). Detail of a ceremonial axe in the name of Ahmose I, treasure of Queen [Ahhotep II](/wiki/Ahhotep_II "Ahhotep II").[[38]](#cite_note-40)

Around 1785 BC, as the power of the Middle Kingdom kings weakened, a [Western Asian](/wiki/Western_Asia "Western Asia") people called the [Hyksos](/wiki/Hyksos "Hyksos"), who had already settled in the Delta, seized control of Egypt and established their capital at [Avaris](/wiki/Avaris "Avaris"), forcing the former central government to retreat to [Thebes](/wiki/Thebes,_Egypt "Thebes, Egypt"). The king was treated as a vassal and expected to pay tribute.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERyholt1997310-41) The Hyksos ('foreign rulers') retained Egyptian models of government and identified as kings, thereby integrating Egyptian elements into their culture.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw2003180–182-42)

After retreating south, the native Theban kings found themselves trapped between the Canaanite Hyksos ruling the north and the Hyksos' [Nubian](/wiki/Nubian_people "Nubian people") allies, the [Kushites](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kush "Kingdom of Kush"), to the south. After years of vassalage, Thebes gathered enough strength to challenge the Hyksos in a conflict that lasted more than 30 years, until 1555 BC.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERyholt1997310-41) [Ahmose I](/wiki/Ahmose_I "Ahmose I") waged a series of campaigns that permanently eradicated the Hyksos' presence in Egypt. He is considered the founder of the [Eighteenth Dynasty](/wiki/Eighteenth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt"), and the military became a central priority for his successors, who sought to expand Egypt's borders and attempted to gain mastery of the [Near East](/wiki/Ancient_Near_East "Ancient Near East").[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERedford1992129,_148–149-43)

### New Kingdom (1549–1069 BC)

Main article: [New Kingdom of Egypt](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Egypt "New Kingdom of Egypt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/CairoEgMuseumTaaMaskMostlyPhotographed.jpg/250px-CairoEgMuseumTaaMaskMostlyPhotographed.jpg)](/wiki/File:CairoEgMuseumTaaMaskMostlyPhotographed.jpg)

Pharaohs' tombs were provided with vast quantities of wealth, such as the [golden mask from the mummy of Tutankhamun](/wiki/Tutankhamun%27s_mask "Tutankhamun's mask").

The New Kingdom [pharaohs](/wiki/Pharaoh "Pharaoh") established a period of unprecedented prosperity by securing their borders and strengthening diplomatic ties with their neighbours, including the [Mitanni](/wiki/Mitanni "Mitanni") Empire, [Assyria](/wiki/Assyria "Assyria"), and [Canaan](/wiki/Canaan "Canaan"). Military campaigns waged under [Tuthmosis I](/wiki/Thutmose_I "Thutmose I") and his grandson [Tuthmosis III](/wiki/Thutmose_III "Thutmose III") extended the influence of the pharaohs to the largest empire Egypt had ever seen.

Between their reigns, [Hatshepsut](/wiki/Hatshepsut "Hatshepsut"), a queen who established herself as pharaoh, launched many building projects, including the restoration of temples damaged by the Hyksos, and sent trading expeditions to [Punt](/wiki/Land_of_Punt "Land of Punt") and the Sinai.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClayton1994104–107-44) When Tuthmosis III died in 1425 BC, Egypt had an empire extending from [Niya](/wiki/Niya_(kingdom) "Niya (kingdom)") in north west [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria") to the [Fourth Cataract](/wiki/Cataracts_of_the_Nile "Cataracts of the Nile") of the Nile in [Nubia](/wiki/Nubia "Nubia"), cementing loyalties and opening access to critical imports such as [bronze](/wiki/Bronze "Bronze") and [wood](/wiki/Wood "Wood").[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJames200548-45)

The New Kingdom pharaohs began a large-scale building campaign to promote the god [Amun](/wiki/Amun "Amun"), whose growing cult was based in [Karnak](/wiki/Karnak "Karnak"). They also constructed monuments to glorify their own achievements, both real and imagined. The Karnak temple is the largest Egyptian temple ever built.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBleiberg200549–50-46)

Around 1350 BC, the stability of the New Kingdom was threatened when Amenhotep IV ascended the throne and instituted a series of radical and chaotic reforms. Changing his name to [Akhenaten](/wiki/Akhenaten "Akhenaten"), he touted the previously obscure [sun deity](/wiki/Solar_deity "Solar deity") [Aten](/wiki/Aten "Aten") as the [supreme deity](/wiki/God "God"), suppressed the worship of most other deities, and moved the capital to the new city of Akhetaten (modern-day [Amarna](/wiki/Amarna "Amarna")).[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAldred1988259-47) He was devoted to his new [religion](/wiki/Atenism "Atenism") and [artistic style](/wiki/Amarna_art "Amarna art"). After his death, the cult of the Aten was quickly abandoned and the traditional religious order restored. The subsequent pharaohs, [Tutankhamun](/wiki/Tutankhamun "Tutankhamun"), [Ay](/wiki/Ay_(pharaoh) "Ay (pharaoh)"), and [Horemheb](/wiki/Horemheb "Horemheb"), worked to erase all mention of Akhenaten's heresy, now known as the [Amarna Period](/wiki/Amarna_Period "Amarna Period").[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'ConnorCline2001273-48)

Around 1279 BC, [Ramesses II](/wiki/Ramesses_II "Ramesses II"), also known as Ramesses the Great, ascended the throne, and went on to build more temples, erect more statues and obelisks, and sire more children than any other pharaoh in history.[[c]](#cite_note-49) A bold military leader, Ramesses II led his army against the Hittites in the [Battle of Kadesh](/wiki/Battle_of_Kadesh "Battle of Kadesh") (in modern [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria")) and, after fighting to a stalemate, finally agreed to the first recorded [peace treaty](/wiki/Egyptian%E2%80%93Hittite_peace_treaty "Egyptian–Hittite peace treaty"), around 1258 BC.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETyldesley200176–77-50)

Egypt's wealth, however, made it a tempting target for invasion, particularly by the [Libyan](/wiki/Ancient_Libya "Ancient Libya") [Berbers](/wiki/Berber_people "Berber people") to the west, and the [Sea Peoples](/wiki/Sea_Peoples "Sea Peoples"), a conjectured confederation of seafarers from the [Aegean Sea](/wiki/Aegean_Sea "Aegean Sea").[[d]](#cite_note-51) Initially, the military was able to [repel](/wiki/Battle_of_Perire "Battle of Perire") these invasions, but Egypt eventually lost control of its remaining territories in southern [Canaan](/wiki/Canaan "Canaan"), much of it falling to the Assyrians. The effects of external threats were exacerbated by internal problems such as corruption, tomb robbery, and [civil unrest](/wiki/End_of_the_19th_Dynasty "End of the 19th Dynasty"). After regaining their power, the high priests at the [temple of Amun](/wiki/Precinct_of_Amun-Re "Precinct of Amun-Re") in Thebes accumulated vast tracts of land and wealth, and their expanded power splintered the country during the Third Intermediate Period.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJames200554-52)

### Third Intermediate Period (1069–653 BC)

Main article: [Third Intermediate Period of Egypt](/wiki/Third_Intermediate_Period_of_Egypt "Third Intermediate Period of Egypt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Rulers_of_Kush%2C_Kerma_Museum.jpg/250px-Rulers_of_Kush%2C_Kerma_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rulers_of_Kush,_Kerma_Museum.jpg)

Statues of two pharaohs of Egypt's [Twenty-Fifth Dynasty](/wiki/Twenty-fifth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Twenty-fifth Dynasty of Egypt") and several other [Kushite](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kush "Kingdom of Kush") kings, [Kerma Museum](/wiki/Kerma_Museum "Kerma Museum")[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBonnet2006128-53)

Following the death of [Ramesses XI](/wiki/Ramesses_XI "Ramesses XI") in 1078 BC, [Smendes](/wiki/Smendes "Smendes") assumed authority over the northern part of Egypt, ruling from the city of [Tanis](/wiki/Tanis,_Egypt "Tanis, Egypt"). The south was effectively controlled by the [High Priests of Amun at Thebes](/wiki/Theban_High_Priests_of_Amun_(21st_and_22nd_Dynasty) "Theban High Priests of Amun (21st and 22nd Dynasty)"), who recognized Smendes in name only.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECerny1975645-54) During this time, Libyans had been settling in the western delta, and chieftains of these settlers began increasing their autonomy. Libyan princes took control of the delta under [Shoshenq I](/wiki/Shoshenq_I "Shoshenq I") in 945 BC, founding the so-called Libyan or Bubastite dynasty that would rule for some 200 years. Shoshenq also gained control of southern Egypt by placing his family members in important priestly positions. Libyan control began to erode as a rival dynasty in the delta arose in [Leontopolis](/wiki/Leontopolis "Leontopolis"), and [Kushites](/wiki/Cushi "Cushi") threatened from the south.

Around 727 BC the Kushite king [Piye](/wiki/Piye "Piye") invaded northward, seizing control of Thebes and eventually the Delta, which established the [25th Dynasty](/wiki/25th_Dynasty "25th Dynasty").[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw2003347-55) During the 25th Dynasty, Pharaoh [Taharqa](/wiki/Taharqa "Taharqa") created an empire nearly as large as the [New Kingdom](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Egypt "New Kingdom of Egypt")'s. Twenty-fifth Dynasty pharaohs built, or restored, temples and monuments throughout the Nile valley, including at Memphis, Karnak, Kawa, and Jebel Barkal.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBonnet2006142–154-56) During this period, the Nile valley saw the first widespread construction of [pyramids (many in modern Sudan)](/wiki/Nubian_pyramids "Nubian pyramids") since the Middle Kingdom.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMokhtar1990161–163-57)[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEmberling20119–11-58)[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESilverman199736–37-59)

Egypt's far-reaching prestige declined considerably toward the end of the Third Intermediate Period. Its foreign allies had fallen into the [Assyrian](/wiki/Assyria "Assyria") sphere of influence, and by 700 BC war between the two states became inevitable. Between 671 and 667 BC the Assyrians began the [Assyrian conquest of Egypt](/wiki/Assyrian_conquest_of_Egypt "Assyrian conquest of Egypt"). The reigns of both [Taharqa](/wiki/Taharqa "Taharqa") and his successor, [Tanutamun](/wiki/Tantamani "Tantamani"), were filled with frequent conflict with the Assyrians. Ultimately, the Assyrians pushed the Kushites back into Nubia, occupied Memphis, and [sacked the temples of Thebes](/wiki/Sack_of_Thebes "Sack of Thebes").[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw2003352–353-60)

### Late Period (653–332 BC)

Main articles: [Late Period of Egypt](/wiki/Late_Period_of_Egypt "Late Period of Egypt") and [History of Persian Egypt](/wiki/History_of_Persian_Egypt "History of Persian Egypt")

The Assyrians left control of Egypt to a series of vassals who became known as the Saite kings of the [Twenty-Sixth Dynasty](/wiki/Twenty-sixth_dynasty_of_Egypt "Twenty-sixth dynasty of Egypt"). By 653 BC, the Saite king [Psamtik I](/wiki/Psamtik_I "Psamtik I") was able to oust the Assyrians with the help of Greek mercenaries, who were recruited to form Egypt's first [navy](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_navy "Ancient Egyptian navy"). [Greek influence](/wiki/Colonies_in_antiquity "Colonies in antiquity") expanded greatly as the [city-state](/wiki/Polis "Polis") of [Naucratis](/wiki/Naucratis "Naucratis") became the home of Greeks in the Nile Delta. The Saite kings based in the new capital of [Sais](/wiki/Sais,_Egypt "Sais, Egypt") witnessed a brief but spirited resurgence in the economy and culture, but in 525 BC, the Persian Empire, led by [Cambyses II](/wiki/Cambyses_II "Cambyses II"), began its conquest of Egypt, eventually defeating the pharaoh [Psamtik III](/wiki/Psamtik_III "Psamtik III") at the [Battle of Pelusium](/wiki/Battle_of_Pelusium "Battle of Pelusium"). Cambyses II then assumed the formal title of pharaoh, but ruled Egypt from Iran, leaving Egypt under the control of a [satrap](/wiki/Satrap "Satrap"). A few revolts against the Persians marked the 5th century BC, but Egypt was never able to overthrow the Persians until the end of the century.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw2003365–369,_374–375,_377-61)

Following its annexation by Persia, Egypt was joined with [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus") and [Phoenicia](/wiki/Phoenicia "Phoenicia") in the sixth satrapy of the [Achaemenid Persian Empire](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire"). This first period of Persian rule over Egypt, also known as the [Twenty-Seventh Dynasty](/wiki/Twenty-Seventh_Dynasty "Twenty-Seventh Dynasty"), ended in 402 BC, when Egypt regained independence under a series of native dynasties. The last of these dynasties, the [Thirtieth](/wiki/Thirtieth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Thirtieth Dynasty of Egypt"), proved to be the last native royal house of ancient Egypt, ending with the kingship of [Nectanebo II](/wiki/Nectanebo_II "Nectanebo II"). A brief restoration of Persian rule, sometimes known as the [Thirty-First Dynasty](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire"), began in 343 BC, but shortly after, in 332 BC, the Persian ruler Mazaces handed Egypt over to [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") without a fight.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw2003377–382-62)

### Ptolemaic period (332–30 BC)

Main article: [Ptolemaic Kingdom](/wiki/Ptolemaic_Kingdom "Ptolemaic Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Ring_with_engraved_portrait_of_Ptolemy_VI_Philometor_%283rd%E2%80%932nd_century_BCE%29_-_2009.jpg/250px-Ring_with_engraved_portrait_of_Ptolemy_VI_Philometor_%283rd%E2%80%932nd_century_BCE%29_-_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ring_with_engraved_portrait_of_Ptolemy_VI_Philometor_(3rd%E2%80%932nd_century_BCE)_-_2009.jpg)

Portrait of [Ptolemy VI Philometor](/wiki/Ptolemy_VI_Philometor "Ptolemy VI Philometor") wearing the [double crown of Egypt](/wiki/Pschent "Pschent")

In 332 BC, [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") conquered Egypt with little resistance from the [Persians](/wiki/Persia "Persia") and was welcomed by the Egyptians as a deliverer. The administration established by Alexander's successors, the [Macedonian](/wiki/Ancient_Macedonians "Ancient Macedonians") [Ptolemaic Kingdom](/wiki/Ptolemaic_Kingdom "Ptolemaic Kingdom"), was based on an Egyptian model and based in the new [capital city](/wiki/Capital_(political) "Capital (political)") of [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria"). The city showcased the power and prestige of Hellenistic rule, and became a centre of learning and culture that included the famous [Library of Alexandria](/wiki/Library_of_Alexandria "Library of Alexandria") and the [Mouseion](/wiki/Mouseion "Mouseion").[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw2003388,_399–400-63) The [Lighthouse of Alexandria](/wiki/Lighthouse_of_Alexandria "Lighthouse of Alexandria") lit the way for the many ships that kept trade flowing through the city—as the [Ptolemies](/wiki/Ptolemaic_dynasty "Ptolemaic dynasty") made commerce and revenue-generating enterprises, such as papyrus manufacturing, their top priority.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw2003405-64)

[Hellenistic culture](/wiki/Hellenistic_civilization "Hellenistic civilization") did not supplant native Egyptian culture, as the Ptolemies supported time-honored traditions in an effort to secure the loyalty of the populace. They built new temples in Egyptian style, supported traditional cults, and portrayed themselves as pharaohs. Some traditions merged, as Greek and [Egyptian gods](/wiki/Egyptian_mythology "Egyptian mythology") were [syncretized](/wiki/Syncretic_religion "Syncretic religion") into composite deities, such as [Serapis](/wiki/Serapis "Serapis"), and [classical Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") forms of sculpture influenced traditional Egyptian motifs. Despite their efforts to appease the Egyptians, the Ptolemies were challenged by native rebellion, bitter family rivalries, and frequent mob violence in Alexandria.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw2003404,_406,_409–412-65) In addition, as [Rome](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") relied more heavily on imports of grain from Egypt, the [Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") took great interest in the political situation in the country. Continued Egyptian revolts, ambitious politicians, and powerful opponents from the Near East made this situation unstable, leading Rome to send forces to secure the country as a province of its empire.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJames200562-66)

### Roman period (30 BC – AD 642)

Main article: [Roman Egypt](/wiki/Roman_Egypt "Roman Egypt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Fayum-22.jpg/250px-Fayum-22.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fayum-22.jpg)

The [Fayum mummy portraits](/wiki/Fayum_mummy_portraits "Fayum mummy portraits") epitomize the meeting of Egyptian and Roman cultures.

Egypt became a province of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") in 30 BC, following the defeat of [Mark Antony](/wiki/Mark_Antony "Mark Antony") and [Ptolemaic](/wiki/Ptolemaic_Kingdom "Ptolemaic Kingdom") Queen [Cleopatra VII](/wiki/Cleopatra_VII "Cleopatra VII") by [Octavian](/wiki/Octavian "Octavian") (later [Emperor](/wiki/Roman_emperor "Roman emperor") Augustus) in the [Battle of Actium](/wiki/Battle_of_Actium "Battle of Actium"). The Romans relied heavily on grain shipments from Egypt, and the [Roman army](/wiki/Roman_army "Roman army"), under the control of a prefect appointed by the emperor, quelled rebellions, strictly enforced the collection of heavy taxes, and prevented attacks by bandits, which had become a notorious problem during the period.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJames200563-67) Alexandria became an increasingly important center on the trade route with the orient, as exotic luxuries were in high demand in Rome.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw2003426-68)

Although the Romans had a more hostile attitude than the Greeks towards the Egyptians, some traditions such as mummification and worship of the traditional gods continued.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw2003422-69) The art of mummy portraiture flourished, and some Roman emperors had themselves depicted as pharaohs, though not to the extent that the Ptolemies had. The former lived outside Egypt and did not perform the ceremonial functions of Egyptian kingship. Local administration became Roman in style and closed to native Egyptians.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw2003422-69)

From the mid-first century AD, [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") took root in Egypt and it was originally seen as another cult that could be accepted. However, it was an uncompromising religion that sought to win converts from the pagan [Egyptian](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_religion "Ancient Egyptian religion") and [Greco-Roman](/wiki/Hellenistic_religion "Hellenistic religion") religions and threatened popular religious traditions. This led to the persecution of converts to Christianity, culminating in the great purges of [Diocletian](/wiki/Diocletian "Diocletian") starting in 303, but eventually Christianity won out.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw2003431-70) In 391, the Christian emperor [Theodosius](/wiki/Theodosius_I "Theodosius I") introduced legislation that banned pagan rites and closed temples.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChadwick2001373-71) Alexandria became the scene of great anti-pagan riots with public and private religious imagery destroyed.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMullen198463-72) As a consequence, Egypt's native religious culture was continually in decline. While the native population continued to speak [their language](/wiki/Egyptian_language "Egyptian language"), the ability to read [hieroglyphic writing](/wiki/Egyptian_hieroglyphs "Egyptian hieroglyphs") slowly disappeared as the role of the Egyptian temple priests and priestesses diminished. The temples themselves were sometimes converted to [churches](/wiki/Church_(building) "Church (building)") or abandoned to the desert.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw2003445-73)

## Government and economy

### Administration and commerce

The pharaoh was the absolute monarch of the country and, at least in theory, wielded complete control of the land and its resources. The king was the supreme [military commander](/wiki/Commander "Commander") and head of the government, who relied on a bureaucracy of officials to manage his affairs. In charge of the administration was his second in command, the [vizier](/wiki/Vizier_(Ancient_Egypt) "Vizier (Ancient Egypt)"), who acted as the king's representative and coordinated land surveys, the treasury, building projects, the legal system, and the [archives](/wiki/Archives "Archives").[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManuelian1998358-74) At a regional level, the country was divided into as many as 42 administrative regions called [nomes](/wiki/Nome_(Egypt) "Nome (Egypt)") each governed by a [nomarch](/wiki/Nomarch "Nomarch"), who was accountable to the vizier for his jurisdiction. The temples formed the backbone of the economy. Not only were they [places of worship](/wiki/Place_of_worship "Place of worship"), but were also responsible for collecting and storing the kingdom's wealth in a system of [granaries](/wiki/Granary "Granary") and treasuries administered by overseers, who redistributed grain and goods.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManuelian1998363-75)

Much of the economy was centrally organized and strictly controlled. Although the ancient Egyptians did not use [coinage](/wiki/Currency "Currency") until the [Late period](/wiki/Late_Period_of_Egypt "Late Period of Egypt"),[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEgypt:_Coins_of_the_Ptolemies2002-76) they did use a type of money-barter system,[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeskell200423-77) with standard sacks of grain and the *[deben](/wiki/Deben_(unit) "Deben (unit)")*, a weight of roughly 91 g (3 oz) of copper or silver, forming a common denominator.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManuelian1998372-78) Workers were paid in grain: A simple laborer might earn 5+1⁄2 sacks or ca. 200 kg (440 lb) of grain per month, while a foreman might earn 7+1⁄2 sacks or roughly 250 kg (550 lb). Prices were fixed across the country and recorded in lists to facilitate trading; for example a shirt cost five copper deben, while a cow cost 140 deben.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManuelian1998372-78) Grain could be traded for other goods, according to the fixed price list.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManuelian1998372-78) During the fifth century BC coined money was introduced into Egypt from abroad. At first the coins were used as standardized pieces of [precious metal](/wiki/Precious_metal "Precious metal") rather than true money, but in the following centuries international traders came to rely on coinage.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurner1984125-79)

### Social status

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Relief_of_a_Nobleman%2C_ca._1295-1070_B.C.E._36.261.jpg/250px-Relief_of_a_Nobleman%2C_ca._1295-1070_B.C.E._36.261.jpg)](/wiki/File:Relief_of_a_Nobleman,_ca._1295-1070_B.C.E._36.261.jpg)

Painted limestone relief of a noble member of Ancient Egyptian society during the New Kingdom

Egyptian society was highly stratified, and [social status](/wiki/Social_status "Social status") was expressly displayed. Farmers made up the bulk of the population, but agricultural produce was owned directly by the state, temple, or [noble family](/wiki/Nobility "Nobility") that owned the land.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManuelian1998383-80) Farmers were also subject to a labor tax and were required to work on irrigation or construction projects in a [corvée](/wiki/Corv%C3%A9e "Corvée") system.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJames2005136-81) Artists and craftsmen were of higher status than farmers, but they were also under state control, working in the shops attached to the temples and paid directly from the state treasury. Scribes and officials formed the upper class in ancient Egypt, known as the "white kilt class" in reference to the bleached linen garments that served as a mark of their rank.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBillard1978109-82) The upper class prominently displayed their social status in art and literature. Below the nobility were the priests, physicians, and engineers with specialized training in their field. It is unclear whether [slavery](/wiki/Slavery "Slavery") as understood today existed in ancient Egypt; there is difference of opinions among authors.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESocial_classes_in_ancient_Egypt2003-83)

The ancient Egyptians viewed men and women, including people from all social classes, as essentially equal under the law, and even the lowliest [peasant](/wiki/Peasant "Peasant") was entitled to petition the [vizier](/wiki/Vizier_(Ancient_Egypt) "Vizier (Ancient Egypt)") and his court for redress.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnson2002-84) Although slaves were mostly used as indentured servants, they were able to buy and sell their servitude, work their way to freedom or nobility, and were usually treated by [doctors](/wiki/Physician "Physician") in the workplace.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESlavery2012-85) Both men and women had the right to own and sell property, make contracts, marry and divorce, receive inheritance, and pursue legal disputes in court. Married couples could own property jointly and protect themselves from divorce by agreeing to marriage contracts, which stipulated the financial obligations of the husband to his wife and children should the marriage end. Compared with their counterparts in ancient Greece, Rome, and even more modern places around the world, ancient Egyptian women had a greater range of personal choices, legal rights, and opportunities for achievement. Women such as [Hatshepsut](/wiki/Hatshepsut "Hatshepsut") and [Cleopatra VII](/wiki/Cleopatra_VII "Cleopatra VII") even became pharaohs, while others wielded power as [Divine Wives of Amun](/wiki/God%27s_Wife_of_Amun "God's Wife of Amun"). Despite these freedoms, [ancient Egyptian women](/wiki/Women_in_ancient_Egypt "Women in ancient Egypt") did not often take part in official roles in the administration, aside from the royal high priestesses, apparently served only secondary roles in the temples (not much data for many dynasties), and were not so probably to be as educated as men.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnson2002-84)

### Legal system

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Louvre-antiquites-egyptiennes-p1020372_Cropped_and_bg_reduced.png/250px-Louvre-antiquites-egyptiennes-p1020372_Cropped_and_bg_reduced.png)](/wiki/File:Louvre-antiquites-egyptiennes-p1020372_Cropped_and_bg_reduced.png)

[The Seated Scribe](/wiki/The_Seated_Scribe "The Seated Scribe") from [Saqqara](/wiki/Saqqara "Saqqara"), [5th dynasty](/wiki/Fifth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Fifth Dynasty of Egypt")

The head of the legal system was officially the pharaoh, who was responsible for enacting laws, delivering justice, and maintaining law and order, a concept the ancient Egyptians referred to as [Ma'at](/wiki/Ma%27at "Ma'at").[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManuelian1998358-74) Although no [legal codes](/wiki/Legal_code "Legal code") from ancient Egypt survive, court documents show that Egyptian law was based on a common-sense view of right and wrong that emphasized reaching agreements and resolving conflicts rather than strictly adhering to a complicated set of statutes.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnson2002-84) Local councils of elders, known as *Kenbet* in the New Kingdom, were responsible for ruling in court cases involving small claims and minor disputes.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManuelian1998358-74) More serious cases involving murder, major land transactions, and tomb robbery were referred to the *Great Kenbet*, over which the vizier or pharaoh presided. Plaintiffs and defendants were expected to represent themselves and were required to swear an oath that they had told the truth. In some cases, the state took on both the role of prosecutor and judge, and it could torture the accused with beatings to obtain a confession and the names of any co-conspirators. Whether the charges were trivial or serious, court scribes documented the complaint, testimony, and verdict of the case for future reference.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOakesGahlin2003472-86)

Punishment for minor crimes involved either imposition of fines, beatings, facial mutilation, or exile, depending on the severity of the offense. Serious crimes such as murder and tomb robbery were punished by execution, carried out by decapitation, drowning, or [impaling](/wiki/Impalement "Impalement") the criminal on a stake. Punishment could also be extended to the criminal's family.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManuelian1998358-74) Beginning in the New Kingdom, [oracles](/wiki/Oracle "Oracle") played a major role in the legal system, dispensing justice in both civil and criminal cases. The procedure was to ask the god a "yes" or "no" question concerning the right or wrong of an issue. The god, carried by a number of priests, rendered judgement by choosing one or the other, moving forward or backward, or pointing to one of the answers written on a piece of papyrus or an [ostracon](/wiki/Ostracon "Ostracon").[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcDowell1999168-87)

### Agriculture

Main article: [Ancient Egyptian agriculture](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_agriculture "Ancient Egyptian agriculture")

See also: [Gardens of ancient Egypt](/wiki/Gardens_of_ancient_Egypt "Gardens of ancient Egypt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Rope_stretching.jpg/250px-Rope_stretching.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rope_stretching.jpg)

Measuring and recording the harvest, from the tomb of [Menna](/wiki/TT69 "TT69") at [Thebes](/wiki/Thebes,_Egypt "Thebes, Egypt") (Eighteenth Dynasty)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Le_Jardin_de_N%C3%A9bamoun.jpg/250px-Le_Jardin_de_N%C3%A9bamoun.jpg)](/wiki/File:Le_Jardin_de_N%C3%A9bamoun.jpg)

Rectangular fishpond with ducks and [lotus](/wiki/Lotus_(plant) "Lotus (plant)") planted round with date palms and fruit trees, [Tomb of Nebamun](/wiki/Tomb_of_Nebamun "Tomb of Nebamun"), Thebes, 18th Dynasty

A combination of favorable geographical features contributed to the success of ancient Egyptian culture, the most important of which was the rich [fertile soil](/wiki/Fertile_soil "Fertile soil") resulting from annual inundations of the Nile River. The ancient Egyptians were thus able to produce an abundance of food, allowing the population to devote more time and resources to cultural, technological, and artistic pursuits. [Land management](/wiki/Land_management "Land management") was crucial in ancient Egypt because taxes were assessed based on the amount of land a person owned.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManuelian1998361-88)

Farming in Egypt was dependent on the cycle of the Nile River. The Egyptians recognized three seasons: *[Akhet](/wiki/Season_of_the_Inundation "Season of the Inundation")* (flooding), *[Peret](/wiki/Season_of_the_Emergence "Season of the Emergence")* (planting), and *[Shemu](/wiki/Season_of_the_Harvest "Season of the Harvest")* (harvesting). The flooding season lasted from June to September, depositing on the river's banks a layer of mineral-rich silt ideal for growing crops. After the floodwaters had receded, the [growing season](/wiki/Growing_season "Growing season") lasted from October to February. Farmers plowed and planted seeds in the fields, which were irrigated with ditches and canals. Egypt received little rainfall, so farmers relied on the Nile to water their crops.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholsonShaw2000514-89) From March to May, farmers used [sickles](/wiki/Sickle "Sickle") to harvest their crops, which were then [threshed](/wiki/Threshing "Threshing") with a [flail](/wiki/Flail_(tool) "Flail (tool)") to separate the straw from the grain. [Winnowing](/wiki/Winnowing "Winnowing") removed the [chaff](/wiki/Chaff "Chaff") from the grain, and the grain was then ground into flour, brewed to make beer, or stored for later use.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholsonShaw2000506-90)

The ancient Egyptians cultivated [emmer](/wiki/Emmer "Emmer") and [barley](/wiki/Barley "Barley"), and several other cereal grains, all of which were used to make the two main food staples of bread and beer.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholsonShaw2000510-91) [Flax](/wiki/Flax "Flax") plants, uprooted before they started flowering, were grown for the fibers of their stems. These fibers were split along their length and spun into thread, which was used to weave sheets of [linen](/wiki/Linen "Linen") and to make clothing. [Papyrus](/wiki/Papyrus "Papyrus") growing on the banks of the Nile River was used to make paper. Vegetables and fruits were grown in garden plots, close to habitations and on higher ground, and had to be watered by hand. Vegetables included leeks, garlic, melons, squashes, pulses, lettuce, and other crops, in addition to grapes that were made into wine.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholsonShaw2000577,_630-92)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Agricultural_Scenes%2C_Tomb_of_Nakht_MET_15.5.19b_detail_2_rgb.jpg/960px-Agricultural_Scenes%2C_Tomb_of_Nakht_MET_15.5.19b_detail_2_rgb.jpg)](/wiki/File:Agricultural_Scenes,_Tomb_of_Nakht_MET_15.5.19b_detail_2_rgb.jpg)

A tomb relief depicts workers plowing the fields, harvesting the crops, and threshing the grain under the direction of an overseer, painting in the tomb of [Nakht](/wiki/TT52 "TT52").

#### Animals

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Maler_der_Grabkammer_des_Sennudem_001.jpg/250px-Maler_der_Grabkammer_des_Sennudem_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maler_der_Grabkammer_des_Sennudem_001.jpg)

[Sennedjem](/wiki/Sennedjem "Sennedjem") plows his fields in [Aaru](/wiki/Aaru "Aaru") with a pair of oxen, [Deir el-Medina](/wiki/Deir_el-Medina "Deir el-Medina").

The Egyptians believed that a balanced relationship between people and [animals](/wiki/Animal "Animal") was an essential element of the cosmic order; thus humans, animals and plants were believed to be members of a single whole.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrouhal1989117-93) Animals, both [domesticated](/wiki/Domestication "Domestication") and [wild](/wiki/Wildlife "Wildlife"), were therefore a critical source of spirituality, companionship, and sustenance to the ancient Egyptians. [Cattle](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_cattle "Ancient Egyptian cattle") were the most important livestock; the administration collected taxes on livestock in regular [censuses](/wiki/Cattle_count "Cattle count"), and the size of a herd reflected the prestige and importance of the estate or temple that owned them. In addition to cattle, the ancient Egyptians kept sheep, goats, and pigs. [Poultry](/wiki/Poultry "Poultry"), such as ducks, geese, and pigeons, were captured in nets and bred on farms, where they were force-fed with dough to fatten them.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManuelian1998381-94) The Nile provided a plentiful source of [fish](/wiki/Fish "Fish"). Bees were also domesticated from at least the Old Kingdom, and provided both honey and wax.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholsonShaw2000409-95)

The ancient Egyptians used donkeys and [oxen](/wiki/Oxen "Oxen") as [beasts of burden](/wiki/Working_animal "Working animal"), and they were responsible for plowing the fields and trampling seed into the soil. The slaughter of a fattened ox was also a central part of an offering ritual. Horses were introduced by the [Hyksos](/wiki/Hyksos "Hyksos") in the [Second Intermediate Period](/wiki/Second_Intermediate_Period "Second Intermediate Period"). Camels, although known from the New Kingdom, were not used as beasts of burden until the Late Period. There is also evidence to suggest that [elephants](/wiki/North_African_elephant "North African elephant") were briefly used in the Late Period but largely abandoned due to lack of [grazing](/wiki/Grazing "Grazing") land.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManuelian1998381-94) [Cats](/wiki/Cats_in_ancient_Egypt "Cats in ancient Egypt"), dogs, and monkeys were common family pets, while more exotic pets imported from the heart of Africa, such as [Sub-Saharan African](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa") [lions](/wiki/Lion "Lion"),[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeptnerSludskii199283–95-96) were reserved for royalty. [Herodotus](/wiki/Herodotus "Herodotus") observed that the Egyptians were the only people to keep their animals with them in their houses.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrouhal1989117-93) During the Late Period, the worship of the gods in their animal form was extremely popular, such as the cat goddess [Bastet](/wiki/Bastet_(mythology) "Bastet (mythology)") and the ibis god [Thoth](/wiki/Thoth "Thoth"), and these animals were kept in large numbers for the purpose of ritual sacrifice.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOakesGahlin2003229-97)

### Natural resources

Further information: [Mining industry of Egypt](/wiki/Mining_industry_of_Egypt "Mining industry of Egypt") and [Stone quarries of ancient Egypt](/wiki/Stone_quarries_of_ancient_Egypt "Stone quarries of ancient Egypt")

Egypt is rich in building and decorative stone, copper and lead ores, gold, and semiprecious stones. These [natural resources](/wiki/Natural_resource "Natural resource") allowed the ancient Egyptians to build monuments, sculpt statues, make tools, and [fashion jewelry](/wiki/Fashion_jewelry "Fashion jewelry").[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreavesLittle1930123-98) [Embalmers](/wiki/Embalming "Embalming") used salts from the [Wadi Natrun](/wiki/Wadi_El_Natrun "Wadi El Natrun") for [mummification](/wiki/Mummy "Mummy"), which also provided the [gypsum](/wiki/Gypsum "Gypsum") needed to make plaster.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELucas1962413-99) Ore-bearing [rock formations](/wiki/Rock_formation "Rock formation") were found in distant, inhospitable [wadis](/wiki/Wadi "Wadi") in the [Eastern Desert](/wiki/Eastern_Desert "Eastern Desert") and the Sinai, requiring large, state-controlled expeditions to obtain natural resources found there. There were extensive [gold mines](/wiki/Gold_mining "Gold mining") in [Nubia](/wiki/Nubia "Nubia"), and one of the first maps known is of a gold mine in this region. The [Wadi Hammamat](/wiki/Wadi_Hammamat "Wadi Hammamat") was a notable source of granite, [greywacke](/wiki/Greywacke "Greywacke"), and gold. [Flint](/wiki/Flint "Flint") was the first mineral collected and used to make tools, and flint handaxes are the earliest pieces of evidence of habitation in the Nile valley. Nodules of the mineral were carefully flaked to make blades and arrowheads of moderate hardness and durability even after copper was adopted for this purpose.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholsonShaw200028-100) Ancient Egyptians were among the first to use minerals such as [sulfur](/wiki/Sulfur "Sulfur") as cosmetic substances.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHogan2011"Sulphur"-101)

The Egyptians worked deposits of the [lead ore](/wiki/Lead "Lead") [galena](/wiki/Galena "Galena") at Gebel Rosas to make net sinkers, plumb bobs, and small figurines. Copper was the most important metal for toolmaking in ancient Egypt and was smelted in furnaces from [malachite](/wiki/Malachite "Malachite") ore mined in the Sinai.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScheel198914-102) Workers collected gold by washing the nuggets out of sediment in [alluvial deposits](/wiki/Alluvium "Alluvium"), or by the more labor-intensive process of grinding and washing gold-bearing quartzite. Iron deposits found in upper Egypt were used in the Late Period.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholsonShaw2000166-103) High-quality building stones were abundant in Egypt; the ancient Egyptians quarried limestone all along the Nile valley, granite from Aswan, and basalt and sandstone from the wadis of the Eastern Desert. Deposits of decorative stones such as [porphyry](/wiki/Porphyry_(geology) "Porphyry (geology)"), greywacke, [alabaster](/wiki/Alabaster "Alabaster"), and [carnelian](/wiki/Carnelian "Carnelian") dotted the Eastern Desert and were collected even before the First Dynasty. In the Ptolemaic and Roman Periods, miners worked deposits of [emeralds](/wiki/Emerald "Emerald") in Wadi Sikait and [amethyst](/wiki/Amethyst "Amethyst") in [Wadi el-Hudi](/wiki/Wadi_el-Hudi "Wadi el-Hudi").[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholsonShaw200051-104)

### Trade

Main article: [Ancient Egyptian trade](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_trade "Ancient Egyptian trade")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Relief_of_Hatshepsut%27s_expedition_to_the_Land_of_Punt_by_%CE%A3%CF%84%CE%B1%CF%8D%CF%81%CE%BF%CF%82.jpg/250px-Relief_of_Hatshepsut%27s_expedition_to_the_Land_of_Punt_by_%CE%A3%CF%84%CE%B1%CF%8D%CF%81%CE%BF%CF%82.jpg)](/wiki/File:Relief_of_Hatshepsut%27s_expedition_to_the_Land_of_Punt_by_%CE%A3%CF%84%CE%B1%CF%8D%CF%81%CE%BF%CF%82.jpg)

Hatshepsut's trading expedition to the [Land of Punt](/wiki/Land_of_Punt "Land of Punt")

The ancient Egyptians engaged in trade with their [foreign neighbors](/wiki/Foreign_contacts_of_ancient_Egypt "Foreign contacts of ancient Egypt") to obtain rare, exotic goods not found in Egypt. In the [Predynastic Period](/wiki/Predynastic_Egypt "Predynastic Egypt"), they established trade with Nubia to obtain gold and incense. They also established trade with Palestine, as evidenced by Palestinian-style oil jugs found in the burials of the First Dynasty pharaohs.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw200363,_69-105) An Egyptian [colony](/wiki/Colony "Colony") stationed in southern [Canaan](/wiki/Canaan "Canaan") dates to slightly before the First Dynasty.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPorat1992433–440-106) [Tell es-Sakan](/wiki/Tell_es-Sakan "Tell es-Sakan") in present-day Gaza was established as an Egyptian settlement in the late 4th millennium BC, and is theorised to have been the main Egyptian colonial site in the region.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_MiroschedjiSadeq2008-107) [Narmer](/wiki/Narmer "Narmer") had Egyptian pottery produced in Canaan and exported back to Egypt.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPorat1986109–129-108)[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEgyptian_pottery_of_the_beginning_of_the_First_Dynasty,_found_in_South_Palestine2000-109)

By the Second Dynasty at latest, ancient Egyptian trade with [Byblos](/wiki/Byblos "Byblos") yielded a critical source of quality timber not found in Egypt. By the Fifth Dynasty, trade with [Punt](/wiki/Land_of_Punt "Land of Punt") provided gold, aromatic resins, ebony, ivory, and wild animals such as monkeys and baboons.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw200374,_101-110) Egypt relied on trade with [Anatolia](/wiki/Anatolia "Anatolia") for essential quantities of tin as well as supplementary supplies of copper, both metals being necessary for the manufacture of bronze. The ancient Egyptians prized the blue stone [lapis lazuli](/wiki/Lapis_lazuli "Lapis lazuli"), which had to be imported from far-away [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"). Egypt's Mediterranean trade partners also included [Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") and Crete, which provided, among other goods, supplies of [olive oil](/wiki/Olive_oil "Olive oil").[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManuelian1998145-111)

## Language

Main article: [Egyptian language](/wiki/Egyptian_language "Egyptian language")

### Historical development

|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| |  |  |  |  |  |  | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | |  |  |  |  |  | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | r Z1 | n | km | m | t O49 | | |
| *r n kmt*  'Egyptian language'  in [hieroglyphs](/wiki/Egyptian_hieroglyphs "Egyptian hieroglyphs") |
|  |
|  |
|  |

The [Egyptian language](/wiki/Egyptian_language "Egyptian language") is a northern [Afro-Asiatic](/wiki/Afro-Asiatic_languages "Afro-Asiatic languages") language closely related to the [Berber](/wiki/Berber_languages "Berber languages") and [Semitic languages](/wiki/Semitic_languages "Semitic languages").[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoprieno1995b2137-112) The Ancient Egyptian language likewise shared linguistic ties with other Afro-Asiatic languages such as the [Chadic languages](/wiki/Chadic_languages "Chadic languages") of west and central Africa, the [Cushitic](/wiki/Cushitic "Cushitic") languages of northeast Africa, and the [Ethio-Semitic languages](/wiki/Ethio-Semitic_languages "Ethio-Semitic languages"), which are found in Ethiopia and Eritrea.[[109]](#cite_note-113)[[110]](#cite_note-114) Many scholars have accepted an African phylum language origin since five of the six Afro-Asiatic subfamilies, including the Egyptian language, are spoken on the African continent, and only one in Asia.[[111]](#cite_note-115)

It has the longest known history of any language having been written from c. 3200 BC to the Middle Ages and remaining as a spoken language for longer. The phases of ancient Egyptian are [Old Egyptian](/wiki/Old_Egyptian "Old Egyptian"), [Middle Egyptian](/wiki/Middle_Egyptian "Middle Egyptian") (Classical Egyptian), [Late Egyptian](/wiki/Late_Egyptian "Late Egyptian"), [Demotic](/wiki/Demotic_Egyptian_language "Demotic Egyptian language") and [Coptic](/wiki/Coptic_language "Coptic language").[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoprieno2004161-116) Egyptian writings do not show dialect differences before Coptic, but it was probably spoken in regional dialects around Memphis and later Thebes.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoprieno2004162-117)

Ancient Egyptian was a [synthetic language](/wiki/Synthetic_language "Synthetic language"), but it became more [analytic](/wiki/Analytic_language "Analytic language") later on. Late Egyptian developed prefixal definite and indefinite [articles](/wiki/Article_(grammar) "Article (grammar)"), which replaced the older inflectional [suffixes](/wiki/Suffix "Suffix"). There was a change from the older [verb–subject–object](/wiki/Verb%E2%80%93subject%E2%80%93object "Verb–subject–object") [word order](/wiki/Word_order "Word order") to [subject–verb–object](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93verb%E2%80%93object "Subject–verb–object").[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoprieno1995b2137–2138-118) The Egyptian [hieroglyphic](/wiki/Egyptian_hieroglyphs "Egyptian hieroglyphs"), [hieratic](/wiki/Hieratic "Hieratic"), and demotic scripts were eventually replaced by the more phonetic [Coptic alphabet](/wiki/Coptic_alphabet "Coptic alphabet"). Coptic is still used in the liturgy of the [Egyptian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Coptic_Orthodox_Church "Coptic Orthodox Church"), and traces of it are found in modern [Egyptian Arabic](/wiki/Egyptian_Arabic "Egyptian Arabic").[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVittman1991197–227-119)

### Sounds and grammar

Ancient Egyptian has 25 consonants similar to those of other Afro-Asiatic languages. These include [pharyngeal](/wiki/Pharyngeal_consonant "Pharyngeal consonant") and [emphatic](/wiki/Emphatic_consonant "Emphatic consonant") consonants, voiced and voiceless stops, voiceless [fricatives](/wiki/Fricative_consonant "Fricative consonant") and voiced and voiceless [affricates](/wiki/Affricate_consonant "Affricate consonant"). It has three long and three short vowels, which expanded in Late Egyptian to about nine.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoprieno1995a46-120) The basic word in Egyptian, similar to Semitic and Berber, is a [triliteral](/wiki/Semitic_root "Semitic root") or biliteral root of consonants and semiconsonants. Suffixes are added to form words. The verb conjugation corresponds to the [person](/wiki/Grammatical_person "Grammatical person"). For example, the triconsonantal skeleton ***S-Ḏ-M*** is the semantic core of the word 'hear'; its basic conjugation is **sḏm**, 'he hears'. If the subject is a noun, suffixes are not added to the verb:[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoprieno1995a74-121) **sḏm ḥmt**, 'the woman hears'.

Adjectives are derived from nouns through a process that Egyptologists call *[nisbation](/wiki/Arabic_grammar "Arabic grammar")* because of its similarity with Arabic.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoprieno2004175-122) The word order is predicate–subject in verbal and adjectival sentences, and subject–predicate in nominal and adverbial sentences.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen200067,_70,_109-123) The subject can be moved to the beginning of sentences if it is long and is followed by a resumptive pronoun.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoprieno1995b2147-124) Verbs and nouns are negated by the [particle](/wiki/Grammatical_particle "Grammatical particle") *n*, but *nn* is used for adverbial and adjectival sentences. [Stress](/wiki/Stress_(linguistics) "Stress (linguistics)") falls on the ultimate or penultimate syllable, which can be open (CV) or closed (CVC).[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoprieno2004173-125)

### Writing

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Rosetta_Stone_BW.jpeg/250px-Rosetta_Stone_BW.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Rosetta_Stone_BW.jpeg)

The [Rosetta Stone](/wiki/Rosetta_Stone "Rosetta Stone") (c. 196 BC) enabled linguists to begin [deciphering ancient Egyptian scripts](/wiki/Decipherment_of_ancient_Egyptian_scripts "Decipherment of ancient Egyptian scripts").[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen200013-126)

[Hieroglyphic writing](/wiki/Egyptian_hieroglyphs "Egyptian hieroglyphs") dates from c. 3000 BC, and is composed of hundreds of symbols. A hieroglyph can represent a word, a sound, or a silent determinative; and the same symbol can serve different purposes in different contexts. Hieroglyphs were a formal script, used on stone monuments and in tombs, that could be as detailed as individual works of art. In day-to-day writing, scribes used a cursive form of writing, called [hieratic](/wiki/Hieratic "Hieratic"), which was quicker and easier. While formal hieroglyphs may be read in rows or columns in either direction (though typically written from right to left), hieratic was always written from right to left, usually in horizontal rows. A new form of writing, [Demotic](/wiki/Demotic_Egyptian_script "Demotic Egyptian script"), became the prevalent writing style, and it is this form of writing—along with formal hieroglyphs—that accompany the Greek text on the Rosetta Stone.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoprieno1995a10–26-127)

Around the first century AD, the Coptic alphabet started to be used alongside the Demotic script. Coptic is a modified [Greek alphabet](/wiki/Greek_alphabet "Greek alphabet") with the addition of some Demotic signs.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen20007-128) Although formal hieroglyphs were used in a ceremonial role until the fourth century, towards the end only a small handful of priests could still read them. As the traditional religious establishments were disbanded, knowledge of hieroglyphic writing was mostly lost. Attempts to decipher them date to the Byzantine[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoprieno2004166-129) and Islamic periods in Egypt,[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEl-Daly2005164-130) but only in the 1820s, after the discovery of the Rosetta Stone and years of research by [Thomas Young](/wiki/Thomas_Young_(scientist) "Thomas Young (scientist)") and [Jean-François Champollion](/wiki/Jean-Fran%C3%A7ois_Champollion "Jean-François Champollion"), were hieroglyphs [substantially deciphered](/wiki/Decipherment_of_ancient_Egyptian_scripts "Decipherment of ancient Egyptian scripts").[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen20008-131)

### Literature

Main article: [Ancient Egyptian literature](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_literature "Ancient Egyptian literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Minnakht_01.JPG/250px-Minnakht_01.JPG)](/wiki/File:Minnakht_01.JPG)

Hieroglyphs on stela in [Louvre](/wiki/Louvre "Louvre"), c. 1321 BC

Writing first appeared in association with kingship on labels and tags for items found in royal tombs. It was primarily an occupation of the scribes, who worked out of the *Per Ankh* institution or the House of Life. The latter comprised offices, libraries (called House of Books), laboratories and observatories.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrouhal1989235-132) Some of the best-known pieces of ancient Egyptian literature, such as the [Pyramid](/wiki/Pyramid_Texts "Pyramid Texts") and [Coffin Texts](/wiki/Coffin_Texts "Coffin Texts"), were written in Classical Egyptian, which continued to be the language of writing until about 1300 BC. Late Egyptian was spoken from the New Kingdom onward and is represented in [Ramesside](/wiki/Ramesside_Period "Ramesside Period") administrative documents, love poetry and tales, as well as in Demotic and Coptic texts. During this period, the tradition of writing had evolved into the tomb autobiography, such as those of [Harkhuf](/wiki/Harkhuf "Harkhuf") and [Weni](/wiki/Weni_the_Elder "Weni the Elder"). The genre known as *[Sebayt](/wiki/Sebayt "Sebayt")* ('instructions') was developed to communicate teachings and guidance from famous nobles; the [Ipuwer papyrus](/wiki/Ipuwer_Papyrus "Ipuwer Papyrus"), a poem of lamentations describing [natural disasters](/wiki/Natural_disaster "Natural disaster") and social upheaval, is a famous example.

The *[Story of Sinuhe](/wiki/Story_of_Sinuhe "Story of Sinuhe")*, written in [Middle Egyptian](/wiki/Middle_Egyptian "Middle Egyptian"), might be the classic of Egyptian literature.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELichtheim197511-133) Also written at this time was the [Westcar Papyrus](/wiki/Westcar_Papyrus "Westcar Papyrus"), a set of stories told to [Khufu](/wiki/Khufu "Khufu") by his sons relating the marvels performed by priests.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELichtheim1975215-134) The [Instruction of Amenemope](/wiki/Instruction_of_Amenemope "Instruction of Amenemope") is considered a masterpiece of Near Eastern literature.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDayGordonWilliamson199523-135) Towards the end of the New Kingdom, the [vernacular language](/wiki/Late_Egyptian "Late Egyptian") was more often employed to write popular pieces such as the [Story of Wenamun](/wiki/Story_of_Wenamun "Story of Wenamun") and the [Instruction of Any](/wiki/Instruction_of_Any "Instruction of Any").
