The former tells the story of a noble who is robbed on his way to buy cedar from Lebanon and of his struggle to return to Egypt. From about 700 BC, narrative stories and instructions, such as the popular Instructions of Onchsheshonqy, as well as personal and business documents were written in the [demotic](/wiki/Demotic_Egyptian_script "Demotic Egyptian script") script and phase of Egyptian. Many stories written in demotic during the [Greco-Roman](/wiki/Greco-Roman "Greco-Roman") period were set in previous historical eras, when Egypt was an independent nation ruled by great pharaohs such as [Ramesses II](/wiki/Ramesses_II "Ramesses II").[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELichtheim1980159-136)

## Culture

### Daily life

Further information: [Clothing in ancient Egypt](/wiki/Clothing_in_ancient_Egypt "Clothing in ancient Egypt"), [Dance in ancient Egypt](/wiki/Dance_in_ancient_Egypt "Dance in ancient Egypt"), and [Music of Egypt § Old Kingdom](/wiki/Music_of_Egypt#Old_Kingdom "Music of Egypt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/LowClassAncientEgyptianStatuettes.png/250px-LowClassAncientEgyptianStatuettes.png)](/wiki/File:LowClassAncientEgyptianStatuettes.png)

Lower-class occupations

Most ancient Egyptians were farmers tied to the land. Their dwellings were restricted to immediate family members, and were constructed of [mudbrick](/wiki/Mudbrick "Mudbrick") designed to remain cool in the heat of the day. Each home had a kitchen with an open roof, which contained a grindstone for milling grain and a small oven for baking the bread.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManuelian1998401-137) [Ceramics](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_pottery "Ancient Egyptian pottery") served as household wares for the storage, preparation, transport, and consumption of food, drink, and raw materials. Walls were painted white and could be covered with dyed linen wall hangings. Floors were covered with reed mats, while wooden stools, beds raised from the floor and individual tables comprised the furniture.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManuelian1998403-138)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Musicians_and_dancers_on_fresco_at_Tomb_of_Nebamun.jpg/250px-Musicians_and_dancers_on_fresco_at_Tomb_of_Nebamun.jpg)](/wiki/File:Musicians_and_dancers_on_fresco_at_Tomb_of_Nebamun.jpg)

Egyptians celebrated feasts and festivals, accompanied by music and dance.

The ancient Egyptians placed a great value on hygiene and appearance. Most bathed in the Nile and used a pasty soap made from [animal fat](/wiki/Animal_fat "Animal fat") and chalk. Men shaved their entire bodies for cleanliness; perfumes and aromatic ointments covered bad odors and soothed skin.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManuelian1998405-139) Clothing was made from simple linen sheets that were bleached white, and both men and women of the upper classes wore wigs, jewelry, and [cosmetics](/wiki/Beauty_and_Cosmetics_in_Ancient_Egypt "Beauty and Cosmetics in Ancient Egypt"). Children went without clothing until maturity, at about age 12, and at this age males were circumcised and had their heads shaved. Mothers were responsible for taking care of the children, while the father provided the family's [income](/wiki/Income "Income").[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManuelian1998406–407-140)

Music and dance were popular entertainments for those who could afford them. Early instruments included flutes and harps, while instruments similar to trumpets, oboes, and pipes developed later and became popular. In the New Kingdom, the Egyptians played on bells, cymbals, tambourines, drums, and imported [lutes](/wiki/Lute "Lute") and [lyres](/wiki/Lyre "Lyre") from Asia.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMusic_in_Ancient_Egypt2003-141) The [sistrum](/wiki/Sistrum "Sistrum") was a rattle-like [musical instrument](/wiki/Musical_instrument "Musical instrument") that was especially important in religious ceremonies.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/ThebesDeirMedinaLandscape.jpg/250px-ThebesDeirMedinaLandscape.jpg)](/wiki/File:ThebesDeirMedinaLandscape.jpg)

Ruins of Deir el-Medina

The ancient Egyptians enjoyed a variety of leisure activities, including games and music. [Senet](/wiki/Senet "Senet"), a board game where pieces moved according to random chance, was particularly popular from the earliest times; another similar game was [mehen](/wiki/Mehen_(game) "Mehen (game)"), which had a circular gaming board. "[Hounds and Jackals](/wiki/Hounds_and_Jackals "Hounds and Jackals")" also known as 58 holes is another example of board games played in ancient Egypt. The first complete set of this game was discovered from a [Theban tomb](/wiki/List_of_Theban_tombs "List of Theban tombs") of the Egyptian pharaoh [Amenemhat IV](/wiki/Amenemhat_IV "Amenemhat IV") that dates to the [13th Dynasty](/wiki/13th_Dynasty "13th Dynasty").[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfe2018Seaburn2018-142) Juggling and [ball games](/wiki/Ball_game "Ball game") were popular with children, and wrestling is also documented in a tomb at [Beni Hasan](/wiki/Beni_Hasan "Beni Hasan").[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManuelian1998126-143) The wealthy members of ancient Egyptian society enjoyed [hunting, fishing](/wiki/Hunting,_fishing_and_animals_in_ancient_Egypt "Hunting, fishing and animals in ancient Egypt"), and boating as well.

The excavation of the workers' village of [Deir el-Medina](/wiki/Deir_el-Medina "Deir el-Medina") has resulted in one of the most thoroughly documented accounts of community life in the ancient world, which spans almost four hundred years. There is no comparable site in which the organization, social interactions, and working and living conditions of a community have been studied in such detail.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHayes1973380-144)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Ancient Egyptian cuisine](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_cuisine "Ancient Egyptian cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Maler_der_Grabkammer_der_Itet_002.jpg/250px-Maler_der_Grabkammer_der_Itet_002.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maler_der_Grabkammer_der_Itet_002.jpg)

Hunting game birds and plowing a field, tomb of [Nefermaat](/wiki/Nefermaat "Nefermaat") and his wife [Itet](/wiki/Itet "Itet") (c. 2700 BC)

Egyptian cuisine remained remarkably stable over time; indeed, the [cuisine of modern Egypt](/wiki/Egyptian_cuisine "Egyptian cuisine") retains some striking similarities to the cuisine of the ancients. The staple diet consisted of bread and beer, supplemented with vegetables such as onions and garlic, and fruit such as dates and figs. Wine and meat were enjoyed by all on feast days while the upper classes indulged on a more regular basis. Fish, meat, and fowl could be salted or dried, and could be cooked in stews or roasted on a grill.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManuelian1998399–400-145)

### Architecture

Main article: [Ancient Egyptian architecture](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_architecture "Ancient Egyptian architecture")

The architecture of ancient Egypt includes some of the most famous structures in the world: the [Great Pyramids of Giza](/wiki/Giza_pyramid_complex "Giza pyramid complex") and the temples at Thebes. Building projects were organized and funded by the state for religious and commemorative purposes, but also to reinforce the wide-ranging power of the pharaoh. The ancient Egyptians were skilled builders; using only simple but effective tools and sighting instruments, architects could build large [stone structures](/wiki/Stone_structures "Stone structures") with great accuracy and precision that is still envied today.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClarkeEngelbach199094–97-146)

The domestic dwellings of elite and ordinary Egyptians alike were constructed from perishable materials such as mudbricks and wood, and have not survived. Peasants lived in simple homes, while the palaces of the elite and the pharaoh were more elaborate structures. A few surviving New Kingdom palaces, such as those in [Malkata](/wiki/Malkata "Malkata") and [Amarna](/wiki/Amarna "Amarna"), show richly decorated walls and floors with scenes of people, birds, water pools, deities and geometric designs.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBadawy196850-147) Important structures such as temples and tombs that were intended to last forever were constructed of stone instead of mudbricks. The architectural elements used in the world's first large-scale stone building, [Djoser](/wiki/Djoser "Djoser")'s mortuary complex, include [post and lintel](/wiki/Post_and_lintel "Post and lintel") supports in the papyrus and lotus motif.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The earliest preserved ancient Egyptian temples, such as those at Giza, consist of single, enclosed halls with roof slabs supported by columns. In the New Kingdom, architects added the [pylon](/wiki/Pylon_(architecture) "Pylon (architecture)"), the open [courtyard](/wiki/Courtyard "Courtyard"), and the enclosed [hypostyle](/wiki/Hypostyle "Hypostyle") hall to the front of the temple's sanctuary, a style that was standard until the Greco-Roman period.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETypes_of_temples_in_ancient_Egypt2003-148) The earliest and most popular tomb architecture in the Old Kingdom was the [mastaba](/wiki/Mastaba "Mastaba"), a flat-roofed rectangular structure of mudbrick or stone built over an underground [burial chamber](/wiki/Chamber_tomb "Chamber tomb"). The [step pyramid of Djoser](/wiki/Pyramid_of_Djoser "Pyramid of Djoser") is a series of stone mastabas stacked on top of each other. Pyramids were built during the Old and Middle Kingdoms, but most later rulers abandoned them in favor of less conspicuous rock-cut tombs.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDodson199123-149) The use of the pyramid form continued in private tomb chapels of the New Kingdom and in the royal [pyramids of Nubia](/wiki/Nubian_pyramids "Nubian pyramids").[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDodsonIkram2008218,_275–276-150)

### Art

Main articles: [Art of ancient Egypt](/wiki/Art_of_ancient_Egypt "Art of ancient Egypt") and [Portraiture in ancient Egypt](/wiki/Portraiture_in_ancient_Egypt "Portraiture in ancient Egypt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Menna_and_Family_Hunting_in_the_Marshes%2C_Tomb_of_Menna_MET_DT10878.jpg/250px-Menna_and_Family_Hunting_in_the_Marshes%2C_Tomb_of_Menna_MET_DT10878.jpg)](/wiki/File:Menna_and_Family_Hunting_in_the_Marshes,_Tomb_of_Menna_MET_DT10878.jpg)

Menna and Family Hunting in the Marshes, Tomb of Menna, c. 1400 BC

The ancient Egyptians produced art to serve functional purposes. For over 3500 years, artists adhered to artistic forms and iconography that were developed during the Old Kingdom, following a strict set of principles that resisted foreign influence and internal change.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobins200829-151) These artistic standards—simple lines, shapes, and flat areas of color combined with the characteristic flat projection of figures with no indication of spatial depth—created a sense of order and balance within a composition. Images and text were intimately interwoven on tomb and temple walls, coffins, stelae, and even statues. The [Narmer Palette](/wiki/Narmer_Palette "Narmer Palette"), for example, displays figures that can also be read as hieroglyphs.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobins200821-152) Because of the rigid rules that governed its highly stylized and symbolic appearance, ancient Egyptian art served its political and religious purposes with precision and clarity.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobins200812-153)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Calcite_Basin_Supporting_a_Boat_with_Shrine00_%286%29.jpg/250px-Calcite_Basin_Supporting_a_Boat_with_Shrine00_%286%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Calcite_Basin_Supporting_a_Boat_with_Shrine00_(6).jpg)

Egyptian [tomb models](/wiki/Wooden_tomb_model "Wooden tomb model") as funerary goods

Ancient Egyptian artisans used stone as a medium for carving statues and fine reliefs, but used wood as a cheap and easily carved substitute. Paints were obtained from minerals such as iron ores (red and yellow ochres), copper ores (blue and green), soot or charcoal (black), and limestone (white). Paints could be mixed with [gum arabic](/wiki/Gum_arabic "Gum arabic") as a binder and pressed into cakes, which could be moistened with water when needed.[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholsonShaw2000105-154)

Pharaohs used [reliefs](/wiki/Relief "Relief") to record victories in battle, royal decrees, and religious scenes. Common citizens had access to pieces of [funerary art](/wiki/Funerary_art "Funerary art"), such as [shabti](/wiki/Ushabti "Ushabti") statues and books of the dead, which they believed would protect them in the afterlife.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJames2005122-155) During the Middle Kingdom, [wooden or clay models](/wiki/Wooden_tomb_model "Wooden tomb model") depicting scenes from everyday life became popular additions to the tomb. In an attempt to duplicate the activities of the living in the [afterlife](/wiki/Afterlife "Afterlife"), these models show laborers, houses, boats, and even military formations that are scale representations of the ideal ancient Egyptian afterlife.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobins200874-156)

Despite the homogeneity of ancient Egyptian art, the styles of particular times and places sometimes reflected changing cultural or political attitudes. After the invasion of the Hyksos in the Second Intermediate Period, [Minoan](/wiki/Minoan_civilization "Minoan civilization")-style frescoes were found in [Avaris](/wiki/Avaris "Avaris").[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw2003204-157) The most striking example of a politically driven change in artistic forms comes from the [Amarna Period](/wiki/Amarna_Period "Amarna Period"), where figures were radically altered to conform to [Akhenaten](/wiki/Akhenaten "Akhenaten")'s revolutionary religious ideas.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobins2008149-158) This style, known as [Amarna art](/wiki/Amarna_art "Amarna art"), was quickly abandoned after Akhenaten's death and replaced by the traditional forms.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobins2008158-159)

### Religious beliefs

Main article: [Ancient Egyptian religion](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_religion "Ancient Egyptian religion")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/The_judgement_of_the_dead_in_the_presence_of_Osiris.jpg/500px-The_judgement_of_the_dead_in_the_presence_of_Osiris.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_judgement_of_the_dead_in_the_presence_of_Osiris.jpg)

The [Book of the Dead](/wiki/Book_of_the_Dead "Book of the Dead") was a guide to the deceased's journey in the afterlife.

Beliefs in the divine and in the afterlife were ingrained in ancient Egyptian civilization from its inception; pharaonic rule was based on the [divine right of kings](/wiki/Divine_Right_of_Kings "Divine Right of Kings"). The Egyptian pantheon was populated by [gods](/wiki/Egyptian_pantheon "Egyptian pantheon") who had supernatural powers and were called on for help or protection. However, the gods were not always viewed as benevolent, and Egyptians believed they had to be appeased with offerings and prayers. The structure of this [pantheon](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_deities "Ancient Egyptian deities") changed continually as new deities were promoted in the hierarchy, but priests made no effort to organize the diverse and sometimes conflicting [myths](/wiki/Egyptian_mythology "Egyptian mythology") and stories into a coherent system.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJames2005102-160) These various conceptions of divinity were not considered contradictory but rather layers in the multiple facets of reality.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERedford2003106-161)

[![Painted relief of a seated man with green skin and tight garments, a man with the head of a jackal, and a man with the head of a falcon](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/La_Tombe_de_Horemheb_cropped.jpg/250px-La_Tombe_de_Horemheb_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:La_Tombe_de_Horemheb_cropped.jpg)

The gods [Osiris](/wiki/Osiris "Osiris"), [Anubis](/wiki/Anubis "Anubis"), and [Horus](/wiki/Horus "Horus") in the tomb of Horemheb ([KV57](/wiki/KV57 "KV57")) in the Valley of the Kings

Gods were worshiped in cult temples administered by priests acting on the king's behalf. At the center of the temple was the cult statue in a shrine. Temples were not places of public worship or congregation, and only on select feast days and celebrations was a shrine carrying the statue of the god brought out for public worship. Normally, the god's domain was sealed off from the outside world and was only accessible to temple officials. Common citizens could worship private statues in their homes, and amulets offered protection against the forces of chaos.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJames2005117-162) After the New Kingdom, the pharaoh's role as a spiritual intermediary was de-emphasized as religious customs shifted to direct worship of the gods. As a result, priests developed a system of [oracles](/wiki/Oracle "Oracle") to communicate the will of the gods directly to the people.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw2003313-163)

The Egyptians believed that every human being was composed of physical and spiritual parts or *aspects*. In addition to the body, each person had a *šwt* (shadow), a *ba* (personality or soul), a *ka* (life-force), and a *name*.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen200079,_94–95-164) The heart, rather than the brain, was considered the seat of thoughts and emotions. After death, the spiritual aspects were released from the body and could move at will, but they required the physical remains (or a substitute, such as a statue) as a permanent home. The ultimate goal of the deceased was to rejoin his *ka* and *ba* and become one of the "blessed dead", living on as an *akh*, or "effective one". For this to happen, the deceased had to be judged worthy in a trial, in which the heart was weighed against a "[feather of truth](/wiki/Maat#Afterlife "Maat")". If deemed worthy, the deceased could continue their existence on earth in spiritual form.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWasserman1994150–153-165) If they were not deemed worthy, their heart was eaten by [Ammit](/wiki/Ammit "Ammit") the Devourer and they were erased from the Universe.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Burial customs

Main article: [Ancient Egyptian funerary practices](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_funerary_practices "Ancient Egyptian funerary practices")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Anubis_attending_the_mummy_of_Sennedjem.jpg/250px-Anubis_attending_the_mummy_of_Sennedjem.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anubis_attending_the_mummy_of_Sennedjem.jpg)

[Anubis](/wiki/Anubis "Anubis"), the god associated with mummification and burial rituals, attending to a mummy

The ancient Egyptians maintained an elaborate set of burial customs that they believed were necessary to ensure immortality after death. These customs involved preserving the body by [mummification](/wiki/Mummy "Mummy"), performing burial ceremonies, and interring with the body goods the deceased would use in the afterlife.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJames2005122-155) Before the Old Kingdom, bodies buried in desert pits were naturally preserved by [desiccation](/wiki/Desiccation "Desiccation"). The arid, desert conditions were a boon throughout the history of ancient Egypt for burials of the poor, who could not afford the elaborate burial preparations available to the elite. Wealthier Egyptians began to bury their dead in stone tombs and use artificial mummification, which involved removing the [internal organs](/wiki/Organ_(anatomy) "Organ (anatomy)"), wrapping the body in linen, and burying it in a rectangular stone sarcophagus or wooden coffin. Beginning in the Fourth Dynasty, some parts were preserved separately in [canopic jars](/wiki/Canopic_jar "Canopic jar").[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIkramDodson199829-166)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Tutankhamun_tomb_photographs_4_326.jpg/250px-Tutankhamun_tomb_photographs_4_326.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tutankhamun_tomb_photographs_4_326.jpg)

A painted, wooden figure found in [Tutankhamun](/wiki/Tutankhamun "Tutankhamun")'s royal tomb

By the New Kingdom, the ancient Egyptians had perfected the art of mummification; the best technique took 70 days and involved removing the internal organs, removing the brain through the nose, and desiccating the body in a mixture of salts called [natron](/wiki/Natron "Natron"). The body was then wrapped in linen with protective amulets inserted between layers and placed in a decorated anthropoid coffin. Mummies of the Late Period were also placed in painted [cartonnage](/wiki/Cartonnage "Cartonnage") mummy cases. Actual preservation practices declined during the Ptolemaic and Roman eras, while greater emphasis was placed on the outer appearance of the mummy, which was decorated.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIkramDodson199840,_51,_138-167)

Wealthy Egyptians were buried with larger quantities of luxury items, but all burials, regardless of social status, included goods for the deceased. [Funerary texts](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_funerary_texts "Ancient Egyptian funerary texts") were often included in the grave, and, beginning in the New Kingdom, so were [shabti](/wiki/Ushabti "Ushabti") statues that were believed to perform manual labor for them in the afterlife.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShabtis2001-168) Rituals in which the deceased was magically re-animated accompanied burials. After burial, living relatives were expected to occasionally bring food to the tomb and recite prayers on behalf of the deceased.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJames2005124-169)

## Military

Main article: [Military of ancient Egypt](/wiki/Military_of_ancient_Egypt "Military of ancient Egypt")

Further information: [Ancient Egyptian navy](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_navy "Ancient Egyptian navy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/King_Tut_charging_enemies%2C_18th_dynasty.jpg/250px-King_Tut_charging_enemies%2C_18th_dynasty.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_Tut_charging_enemies,_18th_dynasty.jpg)

Tutankhamun charging enemies on his [chariot](/wiki/Chariot "Chariot"), 18th dynasty

The ancient Egyptian military was responsible for defending Egypt against foreign invasion, and for maintaining Egypt's domination in the [ancient Near East](/wiki/Ancient_Near_East "Ancient Near East"). The military protected mining expeditions to the Sinai during the Old Kingdom and fought civil wars during the First and Second Intermediate Periods. The military was responsible for maintaining fortifications along important trade routes, such as those found at the city of [Buhen](/wiki/Buhen "Buhen") on the way to Nubia. Forts also were constructed to serve as military bases, such as the fortress at Sile, which was a base of operations for expeditions to the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant"). In the New Kingdom, a series of pharaohs used the standing Egyptian army to attack and conquer [Kush](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kush "Kingdom of Kush") and parts of the Levant.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw2003245-170)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Mesehtisoldiers.JPG/250px-Mesehtisoldiers.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mesehtisoldiers.JPG)

11th Dynasty model of Egyptian soldiers from the tomb of [Mesehti](/wiki/Mesehti "Mesehti"), Upper Egypt

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Nubian_Archers.jpg/250px-Nubian_Archers.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nubian_Archers.jpg)

11th Dynasty model of Nubian archers from a tomb in [Asyut](/wiki/Asyut "Asyut"), Upper Egypt

Typical military equipment included [bows and arrows](/wiki/Bow_(weapon) "Bow (weapon)"), spears, and round-topped shields made by stretching [animal skin](/wiki/Leather "Leather") over a wooden frame. In the New Kingdom, the military began using [chariots](/wiki/Chariotry_in_ancient_Egypt "Chariotry in ancient Egypt") that had earlier been introduced by the Hyksos invaders. Weapons and armor continued to improve after the adoption of bronze: shields were now made from solid wood with a bronze buckle, spears were tipped with a bronze point, and the [khopesh](/wiki/Khopesh "Khopesh") was adopted from Asiatic soldiers.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGutgesell1998365-171) The pharaoh was usually depicted in art and literature riding at the head of the army; it has been suggested that at least a few pharaohs, such as [Seqenenre Tao II](/wiki/Seqenenre_Tao_II "Seqenenre Tao II") and his sons, did do so.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClayton199496-172) However, it has also been argued that "kings of this period did not personally act as frontline war leaders, fighting alongside their troops".[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw2009-173) Soldiers were recruited from the general population, but during, and especially after, the New Kingdom, mercenaries from Nubia, Kush, and Libya were hired to fight for Egypt.[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw2003400-174)

## Technology, medicine and mathematics

### Technology

Main article: [Ancient Egyptian technology](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_technology "Ancient Egyptian technology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Egyptian_glass_jar.jpg/250px-Egyptian_glass_jar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Egyptian_glass_jar.jpg)

Glassmaking was a highly developed art.

In technology, medicine, and mathematics, ancient Egypt achieved a relatively high standard of productivity and sophistication. Traditional [empiricism](/wiki/Empiricism "Empiricism"), as evidenced by the [Edwin Smith](/wiki/Edwin_Smith_Papyrus "Edwin Smith Papyrus") and [Ebers papyri](/wiki/Ebers_Papyrus "Ebers Papyrus") (c. 1600 BC), is first credited to Egypt. The Egyptians created their own alphabet and [decimal system](/wiki/Decimal "Decimal").

### Faience and glass

Even before the Old Kingdom, the ancient Egyptians had developed a glassy material known as [faience](/wiki/Egyptian_faience "Egyptian faience"), which they treated as a type of artificial semi-precious stone. Faience is a non-clay ceramic made of [silica](/wiki/Silicon_dioxide "Silicon dioxide"), small amounts of [lime](/wiki/Calcium_oxide "Calcium oxide") and [soda](/wiki/Sodium_oxide "Sodium oxide"), and a colorant, typically copper.[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholsonShaw2000177-175) The material was used to make beads, tiles, figurines, and small wares. Several methods can be used to create faience, but typically production involved application of the powdered materials in the form of a paste over a clay core, which was then fired. By a related technique, the ancient Egyptians produced a pigment known as [Egyptian blue](/wiki/Egyptian_blue "Egyptian blue"), also called blue frit, which is produced by fusing (or [sintering](/wiki/Sintering "Sintering")) silica, copper, lime, and an alkali such as natron. The product can be ground up and used as a pigment.[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholsonShaw2000109-176)

The ancient Egyptians could fabricate a wide variety of objects from glass with great skill, but it is not clear whether they developed the process independently.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholsonShaw2000195-177) It is also unclear whether they made their own raw glass or merely imported pre-made ingots, which they melted and finished. However, they did have technical expertise in making objects, as well as adding [trace elements](/wiki/Micromineral "Micromineral") to control the color of the finished glass. A range of colors could be produced, including yellow, red, green, blue, purple, and white, and the glass could be made either transparent or opaque.[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholsonShaw2000215-178)

### Medicine

Main article: [Ancient Egyptian medicine](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_medicine "Ancient Egyptian medicine")

The medical problems of the ancient Egyptians stemmed directly from their environment. Living and working close to the Nile brought hazards from [malaria](/wiki/Malaria "Malaria") and debilitating [schistosomiasis](/wiki/Schistosomiasis "Schistosomiasis") parasites, which caused liver and intestinal damage. Dangerous wildlife such as crocodiles and hippos were also a common threat. The lifelong labors of farming and building put stress on the spine and joints, and traumatic injuries from construction and warfare all took a significant toll on the body. The grit and sand from stone-ground flour abraded teeth, leaving them susceptible to [abscesses](/wiki/Abscess "Abscess") (though [caries](/wiki/Dental_caries "Dental caries") were rare).[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiler199594-179)

The diets of the wealthy were rich in sugars, which promoted [periodontal disease](/wiki/Periodontitis "Periodontitis").[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiler199578–80-180) Despite the flattering physiques portrayed on tomb walls, the overweight mummies of many of the upper class show the effects of a life of overindulgence.[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiler199521-181) Adult [life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy") was about 35 for men and 30 for women, but reaching adulthood was difficult as about one-third of the population died in infancy.[[e]](#cite_note-182)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Edwin_Smith_Papyrus_v2.jpg/250px-Edwin_Smith_Papyrus_v2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edwin_Smith_Papyrus_v2.jpg)

The [Edwin Smith surgical papyrus](/wiki/Edwin_Smith_Papyrus "Edwin Smith Papyrus") describes anatomy and medical treatments, written in [hieratic](/wiki/Hieratic "Hieratic"), c. 1550 BC.

Ancient Egyptian physicians were renowned in the ancient Near East for their healing skills, and some, such as [Imhotep](/wiki/Imhotep "Imhotep"), remained famous long after their deaths.[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiler199539-183) Herodotus remarked that there was a high degree of specialization among Egyptian physicians, with some treating only the head or the stomach, while others were eye-doctors and dentists.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrouhal1989243-184) Training of physicians took place at the *Per Ankh* or "House of Life" institution, most notably those headquartered in [Per-Bastet](/wiki/Bubastis "Bubastis") during the New Kingdom and at [Abydos](/wiki/Abydos,_Egypt "Abydos, Egypt") and [Saïs](/wiki/Sais,_Egypt "Sais, Egypt") in the Late period. [Medical papyri](/wiki/Medical_papyri "Medical papyri") show [empirical knowledge](/wiki/Empirical_evidence "Empirical evidence") of anatomy, injuries, and practical treatments.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrouhal1989244–246-185)

Wounds were treated by bandaging with raw meat, white linen, sutures, nets, pads, and swabs soaked with honey to prevent infection,[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrouhal1989250-186) while [opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium"), [thyme](/wiki/Thyme "Thyme"), and [belladona](/wiki/Atropa_belladonna "Atropa belladonna") were used to relieve pain. The earliest records of burn treatment describe burn dressings that use the milk from mothers of male babies. Prayers were made to the goddess [Isis](/wiki/Isis "Isis"). Moldy bread, honey, and copper salts were also used to prevent infection from dirt in burns.[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPećanacJanjićKomarcevićPajić2013263–267-187) Garlic and onions were used regularly to promote good health and were thought to relieve [asthma](/wiki/Asthma "Asthma") symptoms. Ancient Egyptian surgeons stitched wounds, set [broken bones](/wiki/Broken_bones "Broken bones"), and amputated diseased limbs, but they recognized that some injuries were so serious that they could only make the patient comfortable until death occurred.[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiler199538-188)

### Maritime technology

Early Egyptians knew how to assemble planks of wood into a [ship hull](/wiki/Hull_(watercraft) "Hull (watercraft)") and had mastered advanced forms of [shipbuilding](/wiki/Shipbuilding "Shipbuilding") as early as 3000 BC. The [Archaeological Institute of America](/wiki/Archaeological_Institute_of_America "Archaeological Institute of America") reports that the oldest [planked](/wiki/Plank_(wood) "Plank (wood)") [ships](/wiki/Ship "Ship") known are the [Abydos boats](/wiki/Abydos_boats "Abydos boats").[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWard2001-7) A group of 14 discovered ships in [Abydos](/wiki/Abydos,_Egypt "Abydos, Egypt") were constructed of wooden planks "sewn" together. Discovered by Egyptologist David O'Connor of [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University"),[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchuster2000-189) woven [straps](/wiki/Strap "Strap") were found to have been used to lash the planks together,[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWard2001-7) and [reeds](/wiki/Cyperus_papyrus "Cyperus papyrus") or [grass](/wiki/Poaceae "Poaceae") stuffed between the planks helped to seal the seams.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWard2001-7) Because the ships are all buried together and near a mortuary belonging to [Pharaoh Khasekhemwy](/wiki/Khasekhemwy "Khasekhemwy"), originally they were all thought to have belonged to him, but one of the 14 ships dates to 3000 BC, and the associated pottery jars buried with the vessels also suggest earlier dating. The ship dating to 3000 BC was 75 feet (23 m) long and is now thought to perhaps have belonged to an earlier pharaoh, perhaps one as early as [Hor-Aha](/wiki/Hor-Aha "Hor-Aha").[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchuster2000-189)

Early Egyptians also knew how to assemble planks of wood with [treenails](/wiki/Treenail "Treenail") to fasten them together, using [pitch](/wiki/Pitch_(resin) "Pitch (resin)") for [caulking](/wiki/Caulking "Caulking") the seams. The "[Khufu ship](/wiki/Khufu_ship "Khufu ship")", a 43.6-metre (143 ft) vessel sealed into a pit in the Giza pyramid complex at the foot of the [Great Pyramid of Giza](/wiki/Great_Pyramid_of_Giza "Great Pyramid of Giza") in the [Fourth Dynasty](/wiki/Fourth_dynasty_of_Egypt "Fourth dynasty of Egypt") around 2500 BC, is a full-size surviving example that may have filled the symbolic function of a [solar barque](/wiki/Solar_barque "Solar barque"). Early Egyptians also knew how to fasten the planks of this ship together with [mortise and tenon](/wiki/Mortise_and_tenon "Mortise and tenon") joints.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWard2001-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Ancient_Egyptian_Seafaring_Ship.jpg/250px-Ancient_Egyptian_Seafaring_Ship.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ancient_Egyptian_Seafaring_Ship.jpg)

Seagoing ship of an expedition to Punt, from a relief of [Hatshepsut's Mortuary temple](/wiki/Mortuary_temple_of_Hatshepsut "Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut"), Deir el-Bahari

Large seagoing ships are known to have been heavily used by the Egyptians in their trade with the city states of the eastern Mediterranean, especially [Byblos](/wiki/Byblos "Byblos") (on the coast of modern-day Lebanon), and in several expeditions down the Red Sea to the [Land of Punt](/wiki/Land_of_Punt "Land of Punt"). In fact one of the earliest Egyptian words for a seagoing ship is a "Byblos Ship", which originally defined a class of Egyptian seagoing ships used on the Byblos run; however, by the end of the Old Kingdom, the term had come to include large seagoing ships, whatever their destination.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWachsmann200919-190)

In 1977, an ancient north–south canal was discovered extending from [Lake Timsah](/wiki/Lake_Timsah "Lake Timsah") to the Ballah Lakes.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShea197731–38-191) It was dated to the [Middle Kingdom of Egypt](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt") by extrapolating dates of ancient sites constructed along its course.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShea197731–38-191)[[f]](#cite_note-192)

In 2011, archaeologists from Italy, the United States, and Egypt, excavating a dried-up lagoon known as [Mersa Gawasis](/wiki/Mersa_Gawasis "Mersa Gawasis"), unearthed traces of an ancient harbor that once launched early voyages, such as [Hatshepsut](/wiki/Hatshepsut "Hatshepsut")'s Punt, expedition onto the open ocean. Some of the site's most evocative evidence for the ancient Egyptians' seafaring prowess include large ship timbers and hundreds of feet of ropes, made from papyrus, coiled in huge bundles.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurry2011-193) In 2013, a team of Franco-Egyptian archaeologists discovered what is believed to be the world's oldest port, dating back about 4500 years, from the time of King Khufu, on the Red Sea coast, near Wadi el-Jarf (about 110 miles south of [Suez](/wiki/Suez "Suez")).[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoyle2013Lorenzi2013-194)

### Mathematics

Main article: [Ancient Egyptian mathematics](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_mathematics "Ancient Egyptian mathematics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Senenmut-Grab.JPG/250px-Senenmut-Grab.JPG)](/wiki/File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG)

Facsimile of the [Astronomical chart in Senemut's tomb](/wiki/Astronomical_ceiling_of_Senenmut%27s_Tomb "Astronomical ceiling of Senenmut's Tomb"), 18th dynasty[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAstronomical_Ceiling2020-195)

The earliest attested examples of mathematical calculations date to the predynastic [Naqada](/wiki/Naqada "Naqada") period, and show a fully developed [numeral system](/wiki/Egyptian_numerals "Egyptian numerals").[[g]](#cite_note-196) The importance of mathematics to an educated Egyptian is suggested by a New Kingdom fictional letter in which the writer proposes a scholarly competition between himself and another scribe regarding everyday calculation tasks such as accounting of land, labor, and grain.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEImhausen200711-197) Texts such as the [Rhind Mathematical Papyrus](/wiki/Rhind_Mathematical_Papyrus "Rhind Mathematical Papyrus") and the [Moscow Mathematical Papyrus](/wiki/Moscow_Mathematical_Papyrus "Moscow Mathematical Papyrus") show that the ancient Egyptians could perform the four basic mathematical operations—addition, subtraction, [multiplication](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_multiplication "Ancient Egyptian multiplication"), and division—use fractions, calculate the areas of rectangles, triangles, and circles and compute the volumes of boxes, columns and pyramids. They understood basic concepts of [algebra](/wiki/Egyptian_algebra "Egyptian algebra") and [geometry](/wiki/Egyptian_geometry "Egyptian geometry"), and could solve [systems of equations](/wiki/Systems_of_equations "Systems of equations").[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClarkeEngelbach1990222-198) Nubians also exercised a [trigonometric](/wiki/Trigonometry "Trigonometry") methodology comparable to their Egyptian counterparts.[[192]](#cite_note-199)[[193]](#cite_note-200)

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| |  |  | | --- | --- | | |  | | --- | | D22 | | |
| 2⁄3  in [hieroglyphs](/wiki/Egyptian_hieroglyphs "Egyptian hieroglyphs") |
|  |

[Mathematical notation](/wiki/Mathematical_notation "Mathematical notation") was decimal, and based on hieroglyphic signs for each power of ten up to one million. Each of these could be written as many times as necessary to add up to the desired number; so to write the number eighty or eight hundred, the symbol for ten or one hundred was written eight times respectively.[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClarkeEngelbach1990217-201) Because their methods of calculation could not handle most fractions with a numerator greater than one, they had to write [fractions](/wiki/Egyptian_fraction "Egyptian fraction") as the sum of several fractions. For example, they resolved the fraction *two-fifths* into the sum of *one-third* + *one-fifteenth*. Standard tables of values facilitated this.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClarkeEngelbach1990218-202) Some [common fractions](/wiki/Vulgar_fraction "Vulgar fraction"), however, were written with a special glyph—the equivalent of the modern two-thirds is shown on the right.[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGardiner1957197-203)

Ancient Egyptian mathematicians knew the [Pythagorean theorem](/wiki/Pythagorean_theorem "Pythagorean theorem") as an empirical formula. They were aware, for example, that a triangle had a right angle opposite the [hypotenuse](/wiki/Hypotenuse "Hypotenuse") when its sides were in a 3–4–5 ratio.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrouhal1989241-204) They were able to estimate the area of a [circle](/wiki/Circle "Circle") by subtracting one-ninth from its diameter and squaring the result:

:   Area ≈ [(8⁄9)*D*]2 = (256⁄81)*r*2 ≈ 3.16*r*2,

a reasonable approximation of the formula **[π](/wiki/Pi "Pi")***r*2.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrouhal1989241Imhausen200731-205)

## Population

Further information: [Population history of Egypt](/wiki/Population_history_of_Egypt "Population history of Egypt"), [Sahara pump theory](/wiki/Sahara_pump_theory "Sahara pump theory"), and [African humid period](/wiki/African_humid_period "African humid period")

See also: [Ancient Egyptian race controversy](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_race_controversy "Ancient Egyptian race controversy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Megaliths_Aswan_Nubia_museum.JPG/250px-Megaliths_Aswan_Nubia_museum.JPG)](/wiki/File:Megaliths_Aswan_Nubia_museum.JPG)

Megaliths from [Nabta Playa](/wiki/Nabta_Playa "Nabta Playa"), constructed by Neolithic populations, located in [Aswan](/wiki/Aswan "Aswan"), Upper Egypt.[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEhret2023107-206)[[200]](#cite_note-207)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Al_Fayum_arrowheads.png/250px-Al_Fayum_arrowheads.png)](/wiki/File:Al_Fayum_arrowheads.png)

Arrowheads from Al Faiyum, [Lower Egypt](/wiki/Lower_Egypt "Lower Egypt")

Estimates of the size of the population range from 1–1.5 million in the 3rd millennium BC to possibly 2–3 million by the 1st millennium BC, before growing significantly towards the end of that millennium.[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBowman2020-208)

Historical scholarship has generally regarded the peopling of the Egyptian Nile Valley from archaeological and biological data, to be the result of interaction between coastal [northern Africans](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa"), "neolithic" [Saharans](/wiki/African_humid_period "African humid period"), [Nilotic hunters](/wiki/Nilotic_people "Nilotic people"), and riverine proto-[Nubians](/wiki/Nubians "Nubians") with some influence and migration from the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant").[[202]](#cite_note-209)[[203]](#cite_note-210)

International scholarship reflected in the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [General History of Africa](/wiki/General_History_of_Africa "General History of Africa") book series have expressed a similar position. A majority of the scholars that contributed to the Volume II edition (1981) considered Egypt an indigenous African civilisation with a [mixed population](/wiki/Multi-ethnic "Multi-ethnic") that originated largely in the [Sahara](/wiki/Sahara "Sahara") and featured a variety of skin colours from north and south of the Saharan region.[[204]](#cite_note-Africa,_Volume_2_p._462-211)[[205]](#cite_note-212) In the view of Egyptian scholar and featured editor, Gamal Mokhtar, Upper Egypt and Nubia held "similar [ethnic](/wiki/Ethnicity "Ethnicity") composition" with comparable material culture.[[206]](#cite_note-213) An updated Volume IX publication launched in 2025, reaffirmed the view that Egypt had [African](/wiki/Africa "Africa") and [Eurasian](/wiki/Eurasian "Eurasian") populations.[[207]](#cite_note-214) The review section which focused on the 1974 "Peopling of Egypt" symposium stated that accumulated research over three decades had confirmed the migration from [Southernly African](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_African "Sub-Saharan African") along with Saharan populations into the early Nile Valley.[[208]](#cite_note-General_history_of_Africa,_IX:_Gene-215) [Upper Egypt](/wiki/Upper_Egypt "Upper Egypt") was now positioned as a origin point of Pharaonic unification, with supporting [archaeological](/wiki/Archaeology "Archaeology"), [anthropological](/wiki/Biological_anthropology "Biological anthropology"), [genetic](/wiki/Genetics "Genetics") and [linguistic](/wiki/Historical_linguistics "Historical linguistics") sources of evidence having identified close affinities between Upper Egypt and other Sub-Saharan African populations.[[208]](#cite_note-General_history_of_Africa,_IX:_Gene-215)

### Archaeogenetics

Main article: [Genetic history of Egypt](/wiki/Genetic_history_of_Egypt "Genetic history of Egypt")

According to historian William Stiebling and archaeologist Susan N. Helft, conflicting DNA analysis on recent genetic samples such as the [Amarna](/wiki/Amarna "Amarna") royal mummies has led to a lack of consensus on the genetic makeup of the ancient Egyptians and their geographic origins.[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStiebingHelft2023-216)

The genetic history of Ancient Egypt remains a developing field, and is relevant for the understanding of population demographic events connecting Africa and Eurasia. To date, the amount of genome-wide aDNA analyses on ancient specimens from Egypt and Sudan remain scarce, although studies on uniparental haplogroups in ancient individuals have been carried out several times, pointing broadly to affinities with other African and Eurasian groups.[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESirakSawchukPrendergast202220–22-217)[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrendergastSawchukSirak202213-218)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Ancestry_model_of_Nuwayrat_genome_%282855-2570_cal._BCE%29.png/250px-Ancestry_model_of_Nuwayrat_genome_%282855-2570_cal._BCE%29.png)](/wiki/File:Ancestry_model_of_Nuwayrat_genome_(2855-2570_cal._BCE).png)

Ancestry model of Egyptian genome [NUE001](/wiki/Old_Kingdom_individual_(NUE001) "Old Kingdom individual (NUE001)") from Nuwayrat (2855–2570 BC).[[212]](#cite_note-MJ-219)

The currently most advanced full genome analyses was published in a 2025 article by the scientific journal [Nature](/wiki/Nature_(journal) "Nature (journal)"), a whole-genome genetic study of an Old Kingdom adult male Egyptian of relatively high-status, codenamed "[Old Kingdom individual (NUE001)](/wiki/Old_Kingdom_individual_(NUE001) "Old Kingdom individual (NUE001)")", who was radiocarbon-dated to 2855–2570 BC, with funerary practices archeologically attributed to the [Third](/wiki/Third_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Third Dynasty of Egypt") and [Fourth Dynasty](/wiki/Fourth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Fourth Dynasty of Egypt"), excavated in Nuwayrat (Nuerat, نويرات), in a cliff 265 km south of Cairo.[[212]](#cite_note-MJ-219)[[213]](#cite_note-220) Before this study, whole-genome sequencing of ancient Egyptians from the early periods of Egyptian Dynastic history had not yet been accomplished, mainly because of the problematic DNA preservation conditions in Egypt.[[212]](#cite_note-MJ-219) The corpse had been placed intact in a large circular clay pot without embalming, and then installed inside a cliff tomb, which accounts for the comparatively good level of conservation of the skeleton and its DNA.[[212]](#cite_note-MJ-219) Most of his genome was found to be associated with North African Neolithic ancestry, but about 20% of his genetic ancestry could be sourced to the eastern [Fertile Crescent](/wiki/Fertile_Crescent "Fertile Crescent"), including [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia").[[212]](#cite_note-MJ-219) Overall, the 2025 study "provides direct evidence of genetic ancestry related to the eastern Fertile Crescent in ancient Egypt".[[212]](#cite_note-MJ-219) This genetic connection suggests that there had been ancient migration flows from the eastern Fertile Crescent to Egypt, in addition to the exchanges of objects and imagery (domesticated animals and plants, writing systems...) already observed.[[212]](#cite_note-MJ-219) This suggests a pattern of [wide cultural and demographic expansion from the Mesopotamian region](/wiki/Egypt%E2%80%93Mesopotamia_relations "Egypt–Mesopotamia relations"), which affected both Anatolia and Egypt during this period.[[212]](#cite_note-MJ-219) The authors acknowledged some limitations of the study, such as the results deriving from one single Egyptian genome and known limitations predicting specific phenotypic traits in understudied populations.[[214]](#cite_note-221) The analysis also excluded any substantial ancestry in the Nuwayrat genome related to a previously published 4,500-year-old hunter-gatherer genome from the Mota cave in Ethiopia, or other individuals in central, eastern, or southern Africa.[[215]](#cite_note-222)

An earlier partial genomic analyses had been made on much later specimens recovered from the Nile River Valley, Abusir el-Meleq, Egypt, dating from the 787 BC-23 AD time period. Two of the individuals were dated to the Pre-Ptolemaic Period ([New Kingdom](/wiki/New_Kingdom "New Kingdom") to Late Period), and one individual to the Ptolemaic Period. These results point to a genetic continuity of Ancient Egyptians with modern [Egyptians](/wiki/Egyptians "Egyptians"). The results further point to a close genetic affinity between ancient Egyptians and [Middle Eastern](/wiki/Genetic_history_of_the_Middle_East "Genetic history of the Middle East") populations, especially ancient groups from the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant").[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESirakSawchukPrendergast202220–22-217)[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrendergastSawchukSirak202213-218)

Ancient Egyptians also displayed affinities to [Nubians](/wiki/Nubians "Nubians") to the south of Egypt, in modern-day [Sudan](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan"). Archaeological and historical evidence support interactions between Egyptian and Nubian populations more than 5000 years ago, with socio-political dynamics between Egyptians and Nubians ranging from peaceful coexistence to variably successful attempts of conquest. A study on sixty-six ancient Nubian individuals revealed significant contact with ancient Egyptians, characterized by the presence of c. 57% Neolithic/Bronze Age Levantine ancestry in these individuals. Such geneflow of Levantine-like ancestry corresponds with archaeological and botanic evidence, pointing to a Neolithic movement around 7,000 years ago.[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESirakSawchukPrendergast202220–22-217)[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrendergastSawchukSirak202213-218)

Modern Egyptians, like modern Nubians, also underwent subsequent admixture events, contributing both "Sub-Saharan" African-like and West Asian-like ancestries, since the [Roman period](/wiki/Roman_period "Roman period"), with significance on the [African Slave Trade](/wiki/African_Slave_Trade "African Slave Trade") and the [Spread of Islam](/wiki/Spread_of_Islam "Spread of Islam").[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESirakSawchukPrendergast202220–22-217)[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrendergastSawchukSirak202213-218)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/E1b1b.png/250px-E1b1b.png)](/wiki/File:E1b1b.png)

E1b1b is the most common paternal haplogroup across Africa, including Egypt, with modern genetic studies rooting the origin of the E haplogroup in East Africa.[[216]](#cite_note-223)

Genetic analysis of a modern Upper Egyptian population in Adaima by Eric Crubézy had identified genetic markers common across Africa, with 71% of the Adaima samples carrying E1b1 haplogroup and 3% carrying the L0f mitochondrial haplogroup.[[217]](#cite_note-unesdoc.unesco.org-224) A secondary review, published in [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [General History of Africa](/wiki/General_History_of_Africa "General History of Africa") Volume IX, in 2025 noted the results were preliminary and need to be confirmed by other laboratories with new sequencing methods.[[217]](#cite_note-unesdoc.unesco.org-224) This was supported by an anthropological study which found the notable presence of dental markers, characteristic of [Khoisan people](/wiki/Khoisan_people "Khoisan people"), in a predynastic-era cemetery at Adaïma.[[218]](#cite_note-225) The genetic marker [E1b1](/wiki/Haplogroup_E-P2 "Haplogroup E-P2") was identified in a number of genetic studies to have wide distribution across Egypt, with "[P2](/wiki/Haplogroup_E-P2 "Haplogroup E-P2")/[215](/wiki/Haplogroup_E-M215 "Haplogroup E-M215")/[M35.1](/wiki/Haplogroup_E-M35 "Haplogroup E-M35") (E1b1b), for short [M35](/wiki/E-M5 "E-M5"), likely also originated in eastern tropical Africa, and is predominantly distributed in an arc from the Horn of Africa up through Egypt".[[219]](#cite_note-226) Multiple [STR analyses](/wiki/STR_analysis "STR analysis") of the Amarna royal mummies (including Rameses III, Tutankhamun and Amenhotep III) were deployed to estimate their ethnicity have found they had strong affinities with modern Sub-Saharan populations. Nonetheless, these forms of analysis were not exhaustive as only 8 of the 13 [CODIS](/wiki/Combined_DNA_Index_System "Combined DNA Index System") markets were used.[[220]](#cite_note-227)

Some scholars, such as [Christopher Ehret](/wiki/Christopher_Ehret "Christopher Ehret"), caution that a wider sampling area is needed and argue that the current data is inconclusive on the origin of ancient Egyptians. They also point out issues with the previously used methodology such as the sampling size, comparative approach and a "biased interpretation" of the genetic data. They argue in favor for a link between Ancient Egypt and the northern [Horn of Africa](/wiki/Horn_of_Africa "Horn of Africa"). This latter view has been attributed to the corresponding archaeological, genetic, linguistic and biological anthropological sources of evidence which broadly indicate that the earliest Egyptians and Nubians were the descendants of populations in [Northeast Africa](/wiki/Northeast_Africa "Northeast Africa").[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEltisBradleyEngermanPerry2021-228)[[222]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeita2022-229)[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEhret2023-230)[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStiebingHelft2023-216)

### Race and ethnicity

Mainstream scholars have situated the ethnicity and the origins of predynastic, southern Egypt as a foundational community primarily in northeast Africa which included [the Sudan](/wiki/The_Sudan "The Sudan"), [tropical Africa](/wiki/Afrotropical_realm "Afrotropical realm") and [the Sahara](/wiki/The_Sahara "The Sahara") whilst recognising the population variability that became characteristic of the pharaonic period.[[224]](#cite_note-231)[[225]](#cite_note-232)[[226]](#cite_note-233)[[227]](#cite_note-234) Pharaonic Egypt featured a physical [gradation](/wiki/Cline_(biology) "Cline (biology)") across the regional populations, with Upper Egyptians having shared more biological affinities with [Sudanese](/wiki/Sudanese "Sudanese") and [southernly African populations](/wiki/Sub_Saharan_Africa "Sub Saharan Africa"), whereas Lower Egyptians had closer genetic links with [Levantine](/wiki/The_Levant "The Levant") and [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean") populations.[[228]](#cite_note-235)[[229]](#cite_note-236)[[230]](#cite_note-237) In the view of William Stiebling and Susan Helft, "some ancient Egyptians looked more [Middle Eastern](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East") and others looked more [Sudanese](/wiki/Sudanese "Sudanese") or [Ethiopians](/wiki/Ethiopians "Ethiopians") of today, and some may even have looked like other groups in Africa".[[231]](#cite_note-Taylor_&_Francis-238) Overall, the authors reached the conclusion that Ancient Egypt was a heterogeneous civilization with bio-cultural connections across Africa and Eurasia.[[231]](#cite_note-Taylor_&_Francis-238)

Recent studies have emphasized that Ancient Egypt contained much greater [ethnic diversity](/wiki/Ethnic_diversity "Ethnic diversity") than traditionally assumed in earlier historical approaches. Egyptian notions of identity were formed mainly through social constructs, rather than adhering to fixed biological groups. Riggs and Baines note that the common ideological contrast between "Egyptian" and "Other" oversimplifies population groups which varied according to local traditions, dialects, and naming practices.[[232]](#cite_note-239) Archaeological evidence also shows foreigners lived in Egypt, particularly [Nubians](/wiki/Nubians "Nubians"), [Libyans](/wiki/Demographics_of_Libya "Demographics of Libya") and [Asiatics](/wiki/Asian_people "Asian people"). They would frequently participate in activities that which crossed the social divide described by Riggs and Baines. Furthermore, Smith highlights that while state ideology portrayed outsiders through negative stereotypes, evidence regarding everyday interactions, intermarriage, shared foodways, and visual self-presentations demonstrate that ethnic boundaries flexible across social and political contexts.

A number of scholars have argued that Ancient Egyptians shared cultural connections and origins with the [Land of Punt](/wiki/Land_of_Punt "Land of Punt"). This has been accredited to the Egyptian textual descriptions of Puntland as *Ta Nejter* which translates into "God's Land", along with temple reliefs which depicted Puntites with reddish-brown skin complexions similar to their Egyptian counterparts.[[233]](#cite_note-240)[[234]](#cite_note-241)[[235]](#cite_note-242)[[236]](#cite_note-243)

## Legacy

See also: [Egyptian Revival architecture](/wiki/Egyptian_Revival_architecture "Egyptian Revival architecture") and [Tourism in Egypt](/wiki/Tourism_in_Egypt "Tourism in Egypt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/EgyptFrontispiece.jpg/250px-EgyptFrontispiece.jpg)](/wiki/File:EgyptFrontispiece.jpg)

[Frontispiece](/wiki/Book_frontispiece "Book frontispiece") of *[Description de l'Égypte](/wiki/Description_de_l%27%C3%89gypte "Description de l'Égypte")*, published in 38 volumes between 1809 and 1829

The culture and monuments of ancient Egypt have left a lasting legacy on the world. Egyptian civilization significantly influenced the [Kingdom of Kush](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kush "Kingdom of Kush") and [Meroë](/wiki/Mero%C3%AB "Meroë") with both adopting Egyptian religious and architectural norms (hundreds of pyramids (6–30 meters high) were built in Egypt/Sudan), as well as using Egyptian writing as the basis of the [Meroitic script](/wiki/Meroitic_script "Meroitic script").[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETörök199862–67,_299–314,_500–510,_516–527-252) Meroitic is the oldest written language in Africa, other than Egyptian, and was used from the 2nd century BC until the early 5th century AD.[[246]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETörök199862–65-253) The cult of the goddess [Isis](/wiki/Isis "Isis"), for example, became popular in the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"), as obelisks and other relics were transported back to Rome.[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESiliotti19988-254) The Romans also imported [building materials](/wiki/Building_material "Building material") from Egypt to erect Egyptian-style structures. Early historians such as Herodotus, [Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo"), and [Diodorus Siculus](/wiki/Diodorus_Siculus "Diodorus Siculus") studied and wrote about the land, which Romans came to view as a place of mystery.[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESiliotti199810-255)

During the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") and the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"), Egyptian pagan culture was in decline after the rise of Christianity and later [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), but interest in Egyptian antiquity continued in the writings of medieval scholars such as [Dhul-Nun al-Misri](/wiki/Dhul-Nun_al-Misri "Dhul-Nun al-Misri") and [al-Maqrizi](/wiki/Al-Maqrizi "Al-Maqrizi").[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEl-Daly2005112-256) In the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, European travelers and tourists brought back antiquities and wrote stories of their journeys, leading to a wave of [Egyptomania](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt_in_the_Western_imagination "Ancient Egypt in the Western imagination") across Europe, as evident in symbolism such as the [Eye of Providence](/wiki/Eye_of_Providence "Eye of Providence") and the [Great Seal of the United States](/wiki/Great_Seal_of_the_United_States "Great Seal of the United States"). This renewed interest sent collectors to Egypt, who took, purchased, or were given many important antiquities.[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESiliotti199813-257) [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") arranged the first studies in [Egyptology](/wiki/Egyptology "Egyptology") when he brought some 150 scientists and artists to study and document Egypt's [natural history](/wiki/Natural_history "Natural history"), which was published in the *[Description de l'Égypte](/wiki/Description_de_l%27%C3%89gypte "Description de l'Égypte")*.[[251]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESiliotti1998100-258)

In the 20th century, the Egyptian Government and archaeologists alike recognized the importance of cultural respect and integrity in excavations. Since the 2010s, the [Ministry of Tourism and Antiquities](/wiki/Ministry_of_Tourism_and_Antiquities "Ministry of Tourism and Antiquities") has overseen excavations and the recovery of artifacts.[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMohamed2022-259)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** With the death of [Ramesses XI](/wiki/Ramesses_XI "Ramesses XI")
2. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** Depending on the definition, the end of ancient Egypt may be considered to have occurred either with the end of the [Late Period](/wiki/Late_Period_of_Egypt "Late Period of Egypt") in 332 BC or with the end of the [Ptolemaic Kingdom](/wiki/Ptolemaic_Kingdom "Ptolemaic Kingdom") in 30 BC.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-49)** With his two principal wives and large harem, Ramesses II sired more than 100 children. ([Clayton (1994)](#CITEREFClayton1994), p. 146)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-51)** From [Killebrew & Lehmann (2013)](#CITEREFKillebrewLehmann2013), p. 2: "First coined in 1881 by the French Egyptologist G. Maspero (1896), the somewhat misleading term "Sea Peoples" encompasses the ethnonyms Lukka, Sherden, Shekelesh, Teresh, Eqwesh, Denyen, Sikil / Tjekker, Weshesh, and Peleset (Philistines). Footnote: The modern term "Sea Peoples" refers to peoples that appear in several New Kingdom Egyptian texts as originating from "islands"... The use of quotation marks in association with the term "Sea Peoples" in our title is intended to draw attention to the problematic nature of this commonly used term. It is noteworthy that the designation "of the sea" appears only in relation to the Sherden, Shekelesh, and Eqwesh. Subsequently, this term was applied somewhat indiscriminately to several additional ethnonyms, including the Philistines, who are portrayed in their earliest appearance as invaders from the north during the reigns of Merenptah and Ramesses III."

   * From [Drews (1993)](#CITEREFDrews1993), pp. 48–61: "The thesis that a great "migration of the Sea Peoples" occurred ca. 1200 B.C. is supposedly based on Egyptian inscriptions, one from the reign of Merneptah and another from the reign of Ramesses III. Yet in the inscriptions themselves such a migration nowhere appears. After reviewing what the Egyptian texts have to say about 'the sea peoples', one Egyptologist (Wolfgang Helck) recently remarked that although some things are unclear, "eins ist aber sicher: Nach den agyptischen Texten haben wir es nicht mit einer 'Volkerwanderung' zu tun." Thus the migration hypothesis is based not on the inscriptions themselves but on their interpretation."
5. **[^](#cite_ref-182)** Figures are given for *adult* life expectancy and do not reflect life expectancy *at birth.* ([Filer (1995)](#CITEREFFiler1995), p. 25)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-192)** See [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez_Canal "Suez Canal").
7. **[^](#cite_ref-196)** Understanding of Egyptian mathematics is incomplete due to paucity of available material and lack of exhaustive study of the texts that have been uncovered ([Imhausen (2007)](#CITEREFImhausen2007), p. 13).

In general, the inhabitants of Upper Egypt and Nubia had the greatest biological affinity to people of the Sahara and more southerly areas."Lovell, Nancy C. (1999). 226. **[^](#cite_ref-233)** [Ehret (2023)](#CITEREFEhret2023), p. 85: "The physical anthropological findings from the major burial sites of those founding locales of ancient Egypt in the millennium BCE, notably El-Badari as well as Naqada, show no demographic indebtedness to the Levant. They reveal instead a population with cranial and dental features with closest parallels of those of other longtime populations of the surrounding areas of northeastern Africa, such as Nubia and the northern Horn of Africa. Members of this population did not come from somewhere else but were descendants of the long-term inhabitants of these portions of Africa going back many millennia."
227. **[^](#cite_ref-234)** p.355 – "The importance of iconographic sources was emphasized in the main. Säve-Söderbergh and Leclant stressed that the links indicated by cave paintings between the vast expanses of the Sahara and the banks of the Nile nodded to a migration of peoples of the Sahara and groups from the South to the valley –something confirmed by research over the last thirty years. Diop set out to return Egypt to its southern African hinterland by systematically using Pharaonic statues and art to support his point of view. Although a debate on the north-south orientation of a 'civilizing' wave of peoples in the valley had prevailed up to that point, the avalanche of new data now made this idea redundant, suggesting instead the image of a growing and unifying political movement in the valley from south to north that repositioned its starting point back in time: in Upper Egypt, digs at the Uj tomb of King Scorpion at the Abydos necropolis push back the origin of the first Horus back to circa 3250 BCE, and the resumption of excavations at Nekhen led to the exhumation of the famous 'Elephant Kings' of Hierakonpolis (Nekhen) which have no inscriptions and date back even further to circa 3700 BCE."
